AI.motivation: Address the reasoning deficits in masked diffusion language models caused by traditional confidence-based unmasking strategies, which impair logical reasoning by deferring high-entropy tokens.
AI.method: A classification head predicts logical roles for masked positions with high accuracy, followed by a dependency-ordered scheduler for token unmasking based on logical dependency order.
AI.result: LogicDiff improves reasoning accuracy significantly on benchmarks like GSM8K (+38.7 percentage points) and MATH-500 (+5.6 percentage points), with minimal speed overhead.
AI.conclusion: Suboptimal token unmasking, rather than limitations in learned representations, is a key factor in reasoning deficits for masked diffusion language models, and LogicDiff effectively mitigates these issues.

title: SpatialPoint: Spatial-aware Point Prediction for Embodied Localization
summary: Embodied intelligence fundamentally requires a capability to determine where to act in 3D space. We formalize this requirement as embodied localization -- the problem of predicting executable 3D points conditioned on visual observations and language instructions. We instantiate embodied localization with two complementary target types: touchable points, surface-grounded 3D points enabling direct physical interaction, and air points, free-space 3D points specifying placement and navigation goals, directional constraints, or geometric relations. Embodied localization is inherently a problem of embodied 3D spatial reasoning -- yet most existing vision-language systems rely predominantly on RGB inputs, necessitating implicit geometric reconstruction that limits cross-scene generalization, despite the widespread adoption of RGB-D sensors in robotics. To address this gap, we propose SpatialPoint, a spatial-aware vision-language framework with careful design that integrates structured depth into a vision-language model (VLM) and generates camera-frame 3D coordinates. We construct a 2.6M-sample RGB-D dataset covering both touchable and air points QA pairs for training and evaluation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that incorporating depth into VLMs significantly improves embodied localization performance. We further validate SpatialPoint through real-robot deployment across three representative tasks: language-guided robotic arm grasping at specified locations, object placement to target destinations, and mobile robot navigation to goal positions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces 'embodied localization,' where robots predict actionable 3D points based on visual and language inputs, and proposes a depth-integrated vision-language model called SpatialPoint.
AI.motivation: Embodied intelligence needs robots to interact effectively in 3D space, but current models that rely on RGB inputs struggle with spatial reasoning and generalization.
AI.method: The authors developed SpatialPoint, a vision-language framework integrating depth data into the model to generate 3D coordinates, and trained it on a new RGB-D dataset for touchable and air points.
AI.result: Incorporating depth data improved performance in embodied localization tasks, validated by experiments on a 2.6M-sample dataset and robotics applications.
AI.conclusion: SpatialPoint demonstrates its capability in 3D spatial reasoning and real-world robotic tasks, showing that depth-aware vision-language models advance embodied intelligence.

title: Round-trip Engineering for Tactical DDD: A Constraint-Based Vision for the Masses
summary: Despite Domain-Driven Design's proven value in managing complex business logic, a fundamental semantic expressiveness gap persists between generic modeling languages and tactical DDD patterns, causing continuous divergence between design intent and implementation. We envision a constraint-based tactical modeling environment that transforms abstract architectural principles into explicit, tool-enforced engineering constraints. At its core is a DDD-native metamodel where tactical patterns are first-class modeling primitives, coupled with a real-time constraint verification engine that prevents architectural violations during modeling, and bidirectional synchronization mechanisms that maintain model-code consistency through round-trip engineering. This approach aims to democratize tactical DDD by embedding expert-level architectural knowledge directly into modeling constraints, enabling small teams and junior developers to build complex business systems without sacrificing long-term maintainability. By lowering the technical barriers to DDD adoption, we envision transforming tactical DDD from an elite practice requiring continuous expert oversight into an accessible engineering discipline with tool-supported verification.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a DDD-native metamodel and tool-support system to close the gap between modeling languages and tactical Domain-Driven Design (DDD) patterns, ensuring better model-code alignment and easier adoption for non-experts.
AI.motivation: The current gap between generic modeling languages and tactical DDD patterns causes frequent divergence between architectural design and implementation, making DDD difficult to implement effectively without expert oversight.
AI.method: The proposed system includes a constraint-based tactical modeling environment built on a DDD-native metamodel, incorporating real-time constraint verification and bidirectional synchronization between models and code.
AI.result: The approach aims to simplify complex system design for small teams and junior developers, embedding expert-level architectural constraints directly into tools to enable accessible and maintainable DDD practices.
AI.conclusion: By embedding DDD principles at the tool level, this work envisions making tactical DDD a more accessible and democratized practice without compromising on system quality or maintainability.

authors: Wil M. P. van der Aalst
title: Compliance-Aware Predictive Process Monitoring: A Neuro-Symbolic Approach
summary: Existing approaches for predictive process monitoring are sub-symbolic, meaning that they learn correlations between descriptive features and a target feature fully based on data, e.g., predicting the surgical needs of a patient based on historical events and biometrics. However, such approaches fail to incorporate domain-specific process constraints (knowledge), e.g., surgery can only be planned if the patient was released more than a week ago, limiting the adherence to compliance and providing less accurate predictions. In this paper, we present a neuro-symbolic approach for predictive process monitoring, leveraging Logic Tensor Networks (LTNs) to inject process knowledge into predictive models. The proposed approach follows a structured pipeline consisting of four key stages: 1) feature extraction; 2) rule extraction; 3) knowledge base creation; and 4) knowledge injection. Our evaluation shows that, in addition to learning the process constraints, the neuro-symbolic model also achieves better performance, demonstrating higher compliance and improved accuracy compared to baseline approaches across all compliance-aware experiments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a neuro-symbolic approach to predictive process monitoring, integrating domain-specific knowledge using Logic Tensor Networks to improve prediction accuracy and compliance.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to address the limitations of sub-symbolic predictive process monitoring methods, which fail to incorporate domain-specific constraints, resulting in less accurate and compliant predictions.
AI.method: A structured pipeline is introduced with stages for feature extraction, rule extraction, knowledge base creation, and knowledge injection, leveraging Logic Tensor Networks to integrate domain knowledge into predictive models.
AI.result: The proposed neuro-symbolic approach demonstrates improved accuracy and compliance, outperforming baseline methods in experiments designed to evaluate compliance-awareness.
AI.conclusion: Integrating domain-specific knowledge into predictive models enhances their performance and adherence to process constraints, proving the efficacy of the neuro-symbolic approach.

title: A Step Toward Federated Pretraining of Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: The rapid evolution of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) is bottlenecked by the saturation of high-quality public data, while vast amounts of diverse multimodal data remain inaccessible in privacy-sensitive silos. Federated Learning (FL) offers a promising solution to unlock these distributed resources, but existing research focuses predominantly on fine-tuning, leaving the foundational pre-training phase largely unexplored. In this paper, we formally introduce the Federated MLLM Alignment (Fed-MA) task, a lightweight pre-training paradigm that freezes the vision encoder and LLM while collaboratively training the cross-modal projector. We identify two critical challenges in this setting: (i) parameter interference in aggregating local projectors; and (ii) gradient oscillations in one-pass collaborative SGD. To address these challenges, we propose Fed-CMP, a pioneering framework for federated MLLM pre-training. Fed-CMP employs Canonical Reliability-Aware Aggregation, which constructs a canonical space to decompose client projectors into a shared alignment basis and client-specific coefficients, then performs reliability-weighted fusion to suppress parameter interference. Furthermore, Fed-CMP introduces Orthogonality-Preserved Momentum, which applies momentum to the shared alignment basis via orthogonal projection, accumulating historical optimization directions while preserving geometric structure. We construct four federated pre-training scenarios based on public datasets, and extensive experiments validate that Fed-CMP significantly outperforms existing baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a federated pre-training framework for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) called Fed-CMP, which addresses parameter interference and gradient oscillations. Experiments demonstrate its effectiveness.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome the bottleneck of limited high-quality multimodal data for MLLMs by utilizing distributed data in privacy-sensitive silos through Federated Learning.
AI.method: The authors introduce Fed-MA, a lightweight pre-training paradigm freezing vision encoder and LLM while training the cross-modal projector. They propose Fed-CMP, which incorporates Canonical Reliability-Aware Aggregation for reducing parameter interference and Orthogonality-Preserved Momentum to manage gradient oscillations.
AI.result: Fed-CMP demonstrates superior performance over existing methods in federated MLLM pre-training based on evaluations conducted across four public datasets.
AI.conclusion: Fed-CMP provides an innovative approach to federated pre-training, effectively unlocking distributed multimodal data resources and mitigating key challenges in collaborative optimization for MLLMs.

title: Contextual inference from single objects in Vision-Language models
summary: How much scene context a single object carries is a well-studied question in human scene perception, yet how this capacity is organized in vision-language models (VLMs) remains poorly understood, with direct implications for the robustness of these models. We investigate this question through a systematic behavioral and mechanistic analysis of contextual inference from single objects. Presenting VLMs with single objects on masked backgrounds, we probe their ability to infer both fine-grained scene category and coarse superordinate context (indoor vs. outdoor). We found that single objects support above-chance inference at both levels, with performance modulated by the same object properties that predict human scene categorization. Object identity, scene, and superordinate predictions are partially dissociable: accurate inference at one level neither requires nor guarantees accurate inference at the others, and the degree of coupling differs markedly across models. Mechanistically, object representations that remain stable when background context is removed are more predictive of successful contextual inference. Scene and superordinate schemas are grounded in fundamentally different ways: scene identity is encoded in image tokens throughout the network, while superordinate information emerges only late or not at all. Together, these results reveal that the organization of contextual inference in VLMs is more complex than accuracy alone suggests, with behavioral and mechanistic signatures

AI.tldr: This paper investigates contextual inference in vision-language models (VLMs) using single-object images on masked backgrounds, finding that object-level context supports above-chance scene and superordinate category predictions.
AI.motivation: To better understand the capacity and limitations of VLMs in inferring contextual information from single objects, which has implications for their robustness and coherence in scene perception tasks.
AI.method: The authors tested VLMs by presenting them with images of single objects on masked backgrounds and evaluated their ability to infer scene categories and indoor/outdoor (superordinate) contexts. Mechanistic analyses were also performed to examine object representation stability and where contextual information emerges in the models.
AI.result: VLMs achieved above-chance inference of both fine-grained scene categories and superordinate contexts, with performance influenced by object properties similar to those affecting humans. Scene and superordinate predictions were separable, with internal model mechanisms for these tasks differing across networks.
AI.conclusion: Contextual inference in VLMs is influenced by stable object representations and differs by task level. Scene identity is encoded throughout the network, but superordinate context may only emerge late or remain absent.

title: Loop Control Management in Tightly Coupled Processor Arrays (TCPAs)
summary: Multidimensional loop kernels often suffer from control overhead that can dominate execution time on parallel loop accelerators. Tightly Coupled Processor Arrays (TCPAs) offload loop control to a global controller (GC), but existing approaches still require hundreds of control signals. We propose a method to derive and aggressively reduce these control conditions from a polyhedral representation of the iteration space, achieving reductions of 15x to 45x in control signals across several benchmarks. We introduce a lightweight GC architecture that evaluates conditions as unions of polyhedra using bounded evaluation units, requiring hardware comparable to a single processing element. Control signals are distributed throughout the array with a minimal number of delay elements resulting in zero-overhead loop control. Our evaluation on PolyBench kernels shows that the entire control flow requires < 10 % of the total array resources.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to significantly reduce loop control overhead in multidimensional loop kernels using a new global controller design, achieving 15x to 45x reductions in required control signals.
AI.motivation: Control overhead in multidimensional loop kernels often dominates execution time in parallel loop accelerators, creating inefficiency in existing Tightly Coupled Processor Arrays (TCPAs).
AI.method: The authors utilize a polyhedral representation of iteration spaces to derive and reduce control conditions, introduce a lightweight GC architecture, and implement efficient signal distribution.
AI.result: The approach achieves 15x to 45x reductions in control signals and shows that the control flow requires less than 10% of array resources in evaluations using PolyBench kernels.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method significantly optimizes loop control in TCPAs, reducing resource utilization while maintaining loop execution efficiency.

title: RDEx-SOP: Exploitation-Biased Reconstructed Differential Evolution for Fixed-Budget Bound-Constrained Single-Objective Optimization
summary: Bound-constrained single-objective numerical optimisation remains a key benchmark for assessing the robustness and efficiency of evolutionary algorithms. This report documents RDEx-SOP, an exploitation-biased success-history differential evolution variant used in the IEEE CEC 2025 numerical optimisation competition (C06 special session). RDEx-SOP combines success-history parameter adaptation, an exploitation-biased hybrid branch, and lightweight local perturbations to balance fast convergence and final solution quality under a strict evaluation budget. We evaluate RDEx-SOP on the official CEC 2025 SOP benchmark with the U-score framework (Speed and Accuracy categories). Experimental results show that RDEx-SOP achieves strong overall performance and statistically competitive final outcomes across the 29 benchmark functions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RDEx-SOP, a differential evolution algorithm variant, and evaluates its performance on the CEC 2025 benchmark for numerical optimization tasks. Results demonstrate its competitive performance across all test functions.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of balancing fast convergence and solution quality in single-objective numerical optimization under strict evaluation budgets.
AI.method: RDEx-SOP uses success-history parameter adaptation, an exploitation-biased hybrid branch, and lightweight local perturbations to optimize for speed and accuracy. It was tested on the CEC 2025 benchmark using the U-score framework.
AI.result: RDEx-SOP exhibited strong performance across 29 benchmark functions, achieving statistically competitive outcomes in terms of speed and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: RDEx-SOP is effective and robust for numerical optimization, making it a strong candidate for benchmark comparisons in strict evaluation settings.

title: Towards verifying unsafe Rust programs against Rust's pointer-aliasing restrictions
summary: The Rust programming language is famous for its strong ownership regime: at each point, each value is either exclusively owned, exclusively borrowed through a mutable reference, or borrowed as read-only through one or more shared references. These rules, known as Rust's pointer-aliasing rules, are exploited by the Rust compiler to generate more efficient machine code, and enforced by Rust's static type system, except inside unsafe blocks. In this paper, we present our work in progress towards the first program logic for modularly verifying that Rust programs that use unsafe blocks comply with the pointer-aliasing rules.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a modular verification program logic for Rust's pointer-aliasing rules in programs using unsafe blocks.
AI.motivation: To ensure that Rust programs using unsafe blocks comply with the strong pointer-aliasing rules, enhancing safety and performance.
AI.method: The paper progresses in developing a formal program logic for modular verification of unsafe Rust code.
AI.result: Work in progress towards verifying Rust programs with unsafe blocks to uphold pointer-aliasing rules.
AI.conclusion: Establishing a foundational modular verification framework for Rust's unsafe blocks complying with its aliasing rules.

title: On the Loss Landscape Geometry of Regularized Deep Matrix Factorization: Uniqueness and Sharpness
summary: Weight decay is ubiquitous in training deep neural network architectures. Its empirical success is often attributed to capacity control; nonetheless, our theoretical understanding of its effect on the loss landscape and the set of minimizers remains limited. In this paper, we show that $\ell^2$-regularized deep matrix factorization/deep linear network training problems with squared-error loss admit a unique end-to-end minimizer for all target matrices subject to factorization, except for a set of Lebesgue measure zero formed by the depth and the regularization parameter. This observation reveals fundamental properties of the loss landscape of regularized deep matrix factorization problems: the Hessian spectrum is constant across all minimizers of the regularized deep scalar factorization problem with squared-error loss. Moreover, we show that, in regularized deep matrix factorization problems with squared-error loss, if the target matrix does not belong to the Lebesgue measure-zero set, then the Frobenius norm of each layer is constant across all minimizers. This, in turn, yields a global lower bound on the trace of the Hessian evaluated at any minimizer of the regularized deep matrix factorization problem. Furthermore, we establish a critical threshold for the regularization parameter above which the unique end-to-end minimizer collapses to zero.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the impact of weight decay (ℓ2-regularization) in deep matrix factorization, showing unique minimizers and specific properties about the loss landscape and Hessian spectrum, along with thresholds causing collapse to zero.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to deepen the theoretical understanding of how weight decay influences the optimization landscape in deep learning, particularly its effects on minimizers and capacity control.
AI.method: The study investigates the properties of ℓ2-regularized deep linear networks/matrix factorization problems and uses mathematical analysis to explore the loss landscape and minimizer uniqueness for squared-error loss.
AI.result: They demonstrate that end-to-end ℓ2-regularization usually produces a unique minimizer, with a constant Hessian spectrum across minimizers for scalar cases. They also identify a threshold above which the minimizer collapses to zero.
AI.conclusion: The paper uncovers structural insights into ℓ2-regularized matrix factorization problems, showing characteristics that guide the behavior of minimizers, such as unique solutions, consistency in Frobenius norms, and collapse thresholds for regularization strength.

title: Resolving the Robustness-Precision Trade-off in Financial RAG through Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval
comment: 18 pages, 4 figures, 9 tables. Submitted to Expert Systems with Applications
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems for financial document question answering typically follow a chunk-based paradigm: documents are split into fragments, embedded into vector space, and retrieved via similarity search. While effective in general settings, this approach suffers from cross-document chunk confusion in structurally homogeneous corpora such as regulatory filings. Semantic File Routing (SFR), which uses LLM structured output to route queries to whole documents, reduces catastrophic failures but sacrifices the precision of targeted chunk retrieval. We identify this robustness-precision trade-off through controlled evaluation on the FinDER benchmark (1,500 queries across five groups): SFR achieves higher average scores (6.45 vs. 6.02) and fewer failures (10.3% vs. 22.5%), while chunk-based retrieval (CBR) yields more perfect answers (13.8% vs. 8.5%). To resolve this trade-off, we propose Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR), a two-stage architecture that uses SFR as a document filter followed by chunk-based retrieval scoped to the identified document(s). HDRR eliminates cross-document confusion while preserving targeted chunk precision. Experimental results demonstrate that HDRR achieves the best performance on every metric: an average score of 7.54 (25.2% above CBR, 16.9% above SFR), a failure rate of only 6.4%, a correctness rate of 67.7% (+18.7 pp over CBR), and a perfect-answer rate of 20.1% (+6.3 pp over CBR, +11.6 pp over SFR). HDRR resolves the trade-off by simultaneously achieving the lowest failure rate and the highest precision across all five experimental groups.

AI.tldr: For financial document question answering, the paper proposes Hybrid Document-Routed Retrieval (HDRR), which combines Semantic File Routing (SFR) and chunk-based retrieval (CBR), performing best across metrics in regulatory filings benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems face challenges in structurally homogeneous corpora like financial regulatory filings due to cross-document confusion when splitting text into chunks. There is a need for a method that balances robustness and precision.
AI.method: The paper introduces HDRR, a two-stage retrieval system. It first uses Semantic File Routing (SFR) to identify relevant documents, and then applies chunk-based retrieval (CBR) within these documents to retrieve targeted answers.
AI.result: HDRR achieves the best performance on the FinDER benchmark, with an average score of 7.54, lowest failure rate (6.4%), highest correctness rate (67.7%), and highest perfect-answer rate (20.1%), significantly outperforming both SFR and CBR.
AI.conclusion: HDRR effectively resolves the robustness-precision trade-off in financial document QA by combining the strengths of SFR and CBR, outperforming existing systems in both robustness and targeted retrieval precision.

title: Topological Motion Planning Diffusion: Generative Tangle-Free Path Planning for Tethered Robots in Obstacle-Rich Environments
summary: In extreme environments such as underwater exploration and post-disaster rescue, tethered robots require continuous navigation while avoiding cable entanglement. Traditional planners struggle in these lifelong planning scenarios due to topological unawareness, while topology-augmented graph-search methods face computational bottlenecks in obstacle-rich environments where the number of candidate topological classes increases. To address these challenges, we propose Topological Motion Planning Diffusion (TMPD), a novel generative planning framework that integrates lifelong topological memory. Instead of relying on sequential graph search, TMPD leverages a diffusion model to propose a multimodal front-end of kinematically feasible trajectory candidates across various homotopy classes. A tether-aware topological back-end then filters and optimizes these candidates by computing generalized winding numbers to evaluate their topological energy against the accumulated tether configuration. Benchmarking in obstacle-rich simulated environments demonstrates that TMPD achieves a collision-free reach of 100% and a tangle-free rate of 97.0%, outperforming traditional topological search and purely kinematic diffusion baselines in both geometric smoothness and computational efficiency. Simulation with realistic cable dynamics further validates the practicality of the proposed approach.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel planning framework, TMPD, for tethered robots in challenging environments to maintain continuous navigation without cable entanglement. It combines a diffusion model with topological reasoning, achieving superior results over traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Current tethered robot planning faces challenges with topological unawareness in lifelong tasks and computational inefficiencies when using topology-augmented methods in dense obstacle environments.
AI.method: TMPD employs a diffusion-based generative front-end to provide diverse kinematically feasible trajectory candidates across homotopy classes. A topological back-end then assesses and optimizes these candidates for cable entanglement using generalized winding numbers.
AI.result: TMPD achieves 100% collision-free navigation and a 97.0% tangle-free rate, outperforming both topological search and purely kinematic diffusion-based approaches in smoothness and computational speed.
AI.conclusion: TMPD effectively addresses the challenges in tethered robot planning by incorporating lifelong memory and efficient topological evaluation, validated in dense simulated environments and under realistic cable dynamics.

title: Etna: An Evaluation Platform for Property-Based Testing
summary:
Property-based testing is a mainstay of functional programming, boasting a rich literature, an enthusiastic user community, and an abundance of tools~ -- so many, indeed, that new users may have difficulty choosing. Moreover, any given framework may support a variety of strategies for generating test inputs; even experienced users may wonder which are better in any given situation. Sadly, the PBT literature, though long on creativity, is short on rigorous comparisons to help answer such questions.
  We present ETNA, a platform for empirical evaluation and comparison of PBT techniques. ETNA incorporates a number of popular PBT frameworks and testing workloads from the literature, and its extensible architecture makes adding new ones easy, while handling the technical drudgery of performance measurement.
  To illustrate its benefits, we use ETNA to carry out several experiments with popular PBT approaches in Rocq, Haskell, OCaml, Racket, and Rust, allowing users to more clearly understand best practices and tradeoffs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ETNA, a platform for comparing property-based testing (PBT) techniques, addressing the lack of rigorous evaluations in PBT.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of choosing among numerous property-based testing frameworks and strategies by providing a standardized platform for empirical comparison.
AI.method: The authors developed ETNA, an extensible platform that integrates various PBT frameworks and workloads, automating performance measurement and making it easier to evaluate and compare techniques.
AI.result: The study demonstrates the use of ETNA through experiments across popular PBT frameworks (Rocq, Haskell, OCaml, Racket, Rust), providing insights into best practices and trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: ETNA simplifies empirical benchmarking in the PBT domain, promoting deeper understanding and informed decision-making among users regarding frameworks and strategies.

title: Transparency as Architecture: Structural Compliance Gaps in EU AI Act Article 50 II
summary: Art. 50 II of the EU Artificial Intelligence Act mandates dual transparency for AI-generated content: outputs must be labeled in both human-understandable and machine-readable form for automated verification. This requirement, entering into force in August 2026, collides with fundamental constraints of current generative AI systems. Using synthetic data generation and automated fact-checking as diagnostic use cases, we show that compliance cannot be reduced to post-hoc labeling. In fact-checking pipelines, provenance tracking is not feasible under iterative editorial workflows and non-deterministic LLM outputs; moreover, the assistive-function exemption does not apply, as such systems actively assign truth values rather than supporting editorial presentation. In synthetic data generation, persistent dual-mode marking is paradoxical: watermarks surviving human inspection risk being learned as spurious features during training, while marks suited for machine verification are fragile under standard data processing. Across both domains, three structural gaps obstruct compliance: (a) absent cross-platform marking formats for interleaved human-AI outputs; (b) misalignment between the regulation's 'reliability' criterion and probabilistic model behavior; and (c) missing guidance for adapting disclosures to heterogeneous user expertise. Closing these gaps requires transparency to be treated as an architectural design requirement, demanding interdisciplinary research across legal semantics, AI engineering, and human-centered desi

AI.tldr: The EU AI Act's transparency mandate for AI-generated content creates compliance challenges due to current technical limitations in generative AI systems, particularly in synthetic data and fact-checking use cases.
AI.motivation: To investigate the feasibility of complying with the EU AI Act's dual transparency requirement for labeling AI-generated outputs, and to identify where current AI systems fall short.
AI.method: Diagnostic use cases were analyzed, specifically synthetic data generation and automated fact-checking, to explore real-world implications of dual transparency requirements.
AI.result: It was found that post-hoc labeling alone cannot achieve compliance, systemic architectural issues obstruct proper implementation, and gaps exist in marking formats, regulatory alignment, and user adaptability.
AI.conclusion: Achieving compliance requires transparency to become a core architectural design focus, necessitating interdisciplinary collaboration in AI, legal semantics, and human-centered design.

title: Robust Batch-Level Query Routing for Large Language Models under Cost and Capacity Constraints
summary: We study the problem of routing queries to large language models (LLMs) under cost, GPU resources, and concurrency constraints. Prior per-query routing methods often fail to control batch-level cost, especially under non-uniform or adversarial batching. To address this, we propose a batch-level, resource-aware routing framework that jointly optimizes model assignment for each batch while respecting cost and model capacity limits. We further introduce a robust variant that accounts for uncertainty in predicted LLM performance, along with an offline instance allocation procedure that balances quality and throughput across multiple models. Experiments on two multi-task LLM benchmarks show that robustness improves accuracy by 1-14% over non-robust counterparts (depending on the performance estimator), batch-level routing outperforms per-query methods by up to 24% under adversarial batching, and optimized instance allocation yields additional gains of up to 3% compared to a non-optimized allocation, all while strictly controlling cost and GPU resource constraints.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new approach for routing queries to large language models (LLMs) that considers cost, GPU resources, and concurrency constraints, showing significant performance improvements in experimental evaluations.
AI.motivation: Existing per-query routing methods struggle with controlling costs and maintaining resource efficiency, especially under irregular batching conditions, necessitating a more robust batch-aware framework.
AI.method: The authors introduce a resource-aware routing framework that optimizes model assignment at the batch level, incorporates robustness features for uncertain performance predictions, and includes an offline instance allocation procedure.
AI.result: The experiments demonstrate up to 24% improvement in accuracy for batch-level routing over per-query methods under adversarial batching conditions, as well as additional robustness gains of 1-14% and optimized allocation benefits of up to 3%.
AI.conclusion: The proposed batch-level routing framework is both effective and efficient, offering benefits such as improved accuracy, robust performance handling, and precise resource control for large-scale LLM query management.

title: Distilled Large Language Model-Driven Dynamic Sparse Expert Activation Mechanism
summary: High inter-class similarity, extreme scale variation, and limited computational budgets hinder reliable visual recognition across diverse real-world data. Existing vision-centric and cross-modal approaches often rely on rigid fusion mechanisms and heavy annotation pipelines, leading to sub-optimal generalization. We propose the Distilled Large Language Model (LLM)-Driven Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (DS-MoE) framework, which integrates text-guided dynamic routing and lightweight multi-scale comprehension. The DS-MoE framework dynamically aligns textual semantics with defect-specific visual patterns through a sparse MoE architecture, where task-relevant experts are adaptively activated based on semantic relevance, resolving inter-class ambiguity. A lightweight MobileSAM encoder enables real-time inference while preserving multi-scale defect details. Extensive experiments on PCB, aluminum foil, and mold defect datasets demonstrate that our framework achieves superior performance compared to existing pure vision models. \textbf{DS-MoE} surpasses YOLOv8/YOLOX with gains of +13.9, +1.4, and +2.0 pp mAP@ 0.5:0.95 on BBMP, aluminum, and PCB, respectively, while also improving precision and recall.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a novel DS-MoE framework that integrates a sparse mixture-of-experts architecture guided by distilled large language models for addressing visual recognition challenges in defect detection, outperforming existing models on multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: The motivation of this paper is to address the challenges of high inter-class similarity, extreme scale variation, and limited computational budgets in visual recognition for diverse real-world defect detection data.
AI.method: The method involves the DS-MoE framework, which integrates text-guided dynamic routing and lightweight multi-scale comprehension using a sparse mixture-of-experts architecture. It aligns textual semantics with defect-specific visual patterns dynamically, activating task-relevant experts based on semantic relevance. A MobileSAM encoder ensures real-time performance while retaining multi-scale defect details.
AI.result: The proposed framework achieved superior performance on defect detection datasets like PCB, aluminum foil, and mold defects, surpassing YOLOv8/YOLOX with significant mAP improvements (+13.9, +1.4, and +2.0 pp mAP@ 0.5:0.95 ) and enhanced precision/recall.
AI.conclusion: The DS-MoE framework effectively integrates visual and textual semantics to overcome inter-class ambiguity and computational challenges, demonstrating high generalization and efficiency, with potential to improve defect detection systems.

title: Physical Design of UET-RVMCU: A Streamlined Open-Source RISC-V Microcontroller
summary: This paper presents the design and physical implementation of UET-RVMCU, a lightweight RISC-V microcontroller derived from the UETRV-PCore. Aimed at creating an accessible and flexible open-source RISC-V-based microcontroller, UET-RVMCU simplifies the application-class UETRV-PCore by reducing pipeline stages, removing MMU functionality, and integrating GPIO peripherals. The final GDSII layout was generated using an open-source RTL-to-GDS flow (OpenLane). This project demonstrates the feasibility of transforming an application-class SoC into a feature-rich microcontroller suitable for embedded systems, emphasizing low area, design simplicity, and open-source development.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces UET-RVMCU, a lightweight microcontroller based on RISC-V, designed using an open-source flow for embedded systems.
AI.motivation: To develop an accessible, open-source, lightweight, and flexible RISC-V microcontroller catering to embedded systems through modifications of an application-class SoC design.
AI.method: UET-RVMCU was created by simplifying UETRV-PCore through reducing pipeline stages, removing MMU functionality, integrating GPIO peripherals, and finalizing the GDSII layout using the OpenLane RTL-to-GDS flow.
AI.result: The designed UET-RVMCU achieved a low area and simple design while retaining rich features, validating its suitability for embedded systems.
AI.conclusion: The project showcases the feasibility of transforming an application-class SoC into a feature-rich, lightweight, and open-source microcontroller for embedded system applications.

title: RDEx-CSOP: Feasibility-Aware Reconstructed Differential Evolution with Adaptive epsilon-Constraint Ranking
summary: Constrained single-objective numerical optimisation requires both feasibility maintenance and strong objective-value convergence under limited evaluation budgets. This report documents RDEx-CSOP, a constrained differential evolution variant used in the IEEE CEC 2025 numerical optimisation competition (C06 special session). RDEx-CSOP combines success-history parameter adaptation with an exploitation-biased hybrid search and an ε-constraint handling mechanism with a time-varying threshold. We evaluate RDEx-CSOP on the official CEC 2025 CSOP benchmark using the U-score framework (Speed, Accuracy, and Constraint categories). The results show that RDEx-CSOP achieves the highest total score and the best average rank among all released comparison algorithms, mainly through strong speed and competitive constraint-handling performance across the 28 benchmark functions.

AI.tldr: RDEx-CSOP, a constrained differential evolution algorithm, is evaluated in the IEEE CEC 2025 competition and demonstrates top performance in speed and constraint-handling on benchmark functions.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address numerical optimisation challenges by maintaining feasibility and achieving strong objective-value convergence under limited evaluation budgets.
AI.method: RDEx-CSOP integrates success-history parameter adaptation, hybrid search biased toward exploitation, and ε-constraint handling with dynamic thresholds.
AI.result: Evaluation on 28 benchmark functions shows RDEx-CSOP achieving the highest total score and best average rank among comparison algorithms.
AI.conclusion: The results confirm the algorithm's superiority in speed and constraint-handling, making it suitable for constrained single-objective optimisation scenarios.

title: FormalProofBench: Can Models Write Graduate Level Math Proofs That Are Formally Verified?
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop: VerifAI-2: The Second Workshop on AI Verification in the Wild. Live leaderboard hosted here: https://www.vals.ai/benchmarks/proof_bench
summary: We present FormalProofBench, a private benchmark designed to evaluate whether AI models can produce formally verified mathematical proofs at the graduate level. Each task pairs a natural-language problem with a Lean~4 formal statement, and a model must output a Lean proof accepted by the Lean 4 checker. FormalProofBench targets advanced undergraduate and graduate mathematics, with problems drawn from qualifying exams and standard textbooks across topics including analysis, algebra, probability, and logic. We evaluate a range of frontier models with an agentic harness, and find that the best-performing foundation model achieves 33.5% accuracy, with performance dropping rapidly after that. In addition to the accuracy numbers, we also provide empirical analysis of tool-use, failure modes, cost and latency, thereby providing a thorough evaluation of the formal-theorem proving abilities of frontier models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces FormalProofBench, a benchmark for testing AI's ability to produce formally verified mathematical proofs with Lean 4, targeting advanced undergraduate and graduate-level math.
AI.motivation: The goal is to evaluate contemporary AI models' capabilities in generating valid formal proofs for complex mathematical problems, an essential task in advancing artificial intelligence's understanding of formal reasoning.
AI.method: They created a benchmark consisting of natural-language problems paired with corresponding formal statements in Lean 4. Evaluations were performed using frontier AI models integrated with an agentic harness.
AI.result: The top AI model achieved 33.5% accuracy, with performance decreasing for others. Additionally, the authors analyzed factors such as tool usage, failure modes, costs, and latency.
AI.conclusion: The study highlights that while AI has made progress in formal proof generation, current models are far from mastering graduate-level mathematics, suggesting a need for further advancements.

title: Optimising Blockchain Scalability for Real-Time IoT Applications
summary: The convergence of blockchain and the Internet of Things (IoT) enables secure, decentralised, and verifiable data exchange across distributed smart environments. However, traditional blockchain frameworks suffer from inherent scalability constraints, limited throughput, and high latency, which conflict with the stringent real-time requirements of IoT applications such as industrial automation, intelligent healthcare, and smart transportation. These systems demand ultra-low latency, high transaction throughput, lightweight computation, and efficient resource utilisation. This review provides a comprehensive, structured analysis of state-of-the-art scalability solutions specifically adapted to blockchain-enabled IoT. The discussion encompasses Layer 1 enhancements, Layer 2 off-chain processing, sharding-based parallelisation, integration of edge and fog computing, and hybrid consensus mechanisms. For each approach, the review highlights operational principles, performance benefits, trade-offs in decentralisation and security, and suitability for latency-sensitive deployments. Furthermore, real-time quality-of-service considerations are examined to understand how scalability strategies impact system responsiveness, energy efficiency, and data integrity. Key open challenges, including the scalability-security trade-off, privacy preservation, interoperability, and sustainable resource management, have been identified as persistent barriers to large-scale adoption. Finally, the review outlines future research directions, emphasising adaptive and AI-driven consensus algorithms, quantum-safe cryptographic models, the convergence of blockchain with 5G/6G networks, and edge intelligence. By consolidating diverse technical insights and emerging trends, this work serves as a timely reference for developing scalable, secure, and sustainable blockchain architectures for real-time IoT applications.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews scalability solutions for blockchain-enabled IoT applications addressing latency, throughput, and resource efficiency limitations.
AI.motivation: To address the scalability issues in traditional blockchain frameworks that hinder their application in IoT environments demanding real-time and efficient data handling.
AI.method: A structured review of various scalability solutions including Layer 1, Layer 2, sharding, edge computing, and hybrid consensus mechanisms.
AI.result: The paper critiques the operational principles, performance benefits, security trade-offs, and suitability of these solutions for latency-sensitive IoT needs.
AI.conclusion: Future research should focus on AI-driven consensus, quantum-safe cryptography, integration with next-gen networks, and edge intelligence to overcome current challenges in scalability, security, and sustainability of blockchain for IoT.

title: Bayes-MICE: A Bayesian Approach to Multiple Imputation for Time Series Data
summary:
Time-series analysis is often affected by missing data, a common problem across several fields, including healthcare and environmental monitoring.
  Multiple Imputation by Chained Equations (MICE) has been prominent for imputing missing values through "fully conditional specification". We extend MICE using the Bayesian framework (Bayes-MICE), utilising Bayesian inference to impute missing values via Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to account for uncertainty in MICE model parameters and imputed values. We also include temporally informed initialisation and time-lagged features in the model to respect the sequential nature of time-series data. We evaluate the Bayes-MICE method using two real-world datasets (AirQuality and PhysioNet), and using both the Random Walk Metropolis (RWM) and the Metropolis-Adjusted Langevin Algorithm (MALA) samplers. Our results demonstrate that Bayes-MICE reduces imputation errors relative to the baseline methods over all variables and accounts for uncertainty in the imputation process, thereby providing a more accurate measure of imputation error. We also found that MALA converges faster than RWM, achieving comparable accuracy while providing more consistent posterior exploration. Overall, these findings suggest that the Bayes-MICE framework represents a practical and efficient approach to time-series imputation, balancing increased accuracy with meaningful quantification of uncertainty in various environmental and clinical settings.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Bayes-MICE, an extension of MICE for time-series imputation, which uses Bayesian inference and temporal features to improve accuracy and quantify uncertainty in missing data imputation.
AI.motivation: Missing data is a challenging problem in time-series analysis across various domains. Current imputation techniques often fail to incorporate uncertainty and temporal structure effectively.
AI.method: The authors propose Bayes-MICE, which combines Bayesian inference with the MICE framework. This approach uses MCMC sampling, temporal initialization, and time-lagged features for imputing missing data in time-series datasets.
AI.result: The method, evaluated using AirQuality and PhysioNet datasets, shows reduced imputation errors and incorporates uncertainty into the process. MALA sampler outperforms RWM in terms of convergence speed while maintaining similar imputation accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Bayes-MICE is an efficient and accurate method for time-series imputation that accommodates uncertainty and can be applied in domains like healthcare and environmental monitoring.

title: Implicit neural representations for larval zebrafish brain microscopy: a reproducible benchmark on the MapZebrain atlas
summary: Implicit neural representations (INRs) offer continuous coordinate-based encodings for atlas registration, cross-modality resampling, sparse-view completion, and compact sharing of neuroanatomical data. Yet reproducible evaluation is lacking for high-resolution larval zebrafish microscopy, where preserving neuropil boundaries and fine neuronal processes is critical. We present a reproducible INR benchmark for the MapZebrain larval zebrafish brain atlas. Using a unified, seed-controlled protocol, we compare SIREN, Fourier features, Haar positional encoding, and a multi-resolution grid on 950 grayscale microscopy images, including atlas slices and single-neuron projections. Images are normalized with per-image (1,99) percentiles estimated from 10% of pixels in non-held-out columns, and spatial generalization is tested with a deterministic 40% column-wise hold-out along the X-axis. Haar and Fourier achieve the strongest macro-averaged reconstruction fidelity on held-out columns (about 26 dB), while the grid is moderately behind. SIREN performs worse in macro averages but remains competitive on area-weighted micro averages in the all-in-one regime. SSIM and edge-focused error further show that Haar and Fourier preserve boundaries more accurately. These results indicate that explicit spectral and multiscale encodings better capture high-frequency neuroanatomical detail than smoother-bias alternatives. For MapZebrain workflows, Haar and Fourier are best suited to boundary-sensitive tasks such as atlas registration, label transfer, and morphology-preserving sharing, while SIREN remains a lightweight baseline for background modelling or denoising.

AI.tldr: This paper benchmarks different implicit neural representation (INR) methods for high-resolution larval zebrafish brain microscopy, focusing on reconstruction fidelity and boundary preservation.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of reproducible evaluation frameworks for INRs in high-resolution larval zebrafish microscopy, especially for preserving neuroanatomical details like neuropil boundaries and fine neuronal structures.
AI.method: The authors benchmarked SIREN, Fourier features, Haar positional encoding, and multi-resolution grids on 950 grayscale microscopy images. They used a reproducible protocol, normalized images, and tested spatial generalization with a 40% column-wise hold-out along the X-axis.
AI.result: Haar and Fourier achieved the best macro-averaged reconstruction fidelity (~26 dB) for held-out columns, excelling in boundary preservation. SIREN showed weaker macro-averages but performed decently on area-weighted micro averages. Edge-sensitive metrics confirmed Haar and Fourier's superiority for boundary-sensitive tasks.
AI.conclusion: Haar and Fourier are ideal for boundary-sensitive applications like atlas registration and label transfer in zebrafish neuroanatomy, while SIREN is better suited for lightweight tasks like background modeling or denoising.

title: Arithmetic OOD Failure Unfolds in Stages in Minimal GPTs
summary: Arithmetic benchmarks are often reduced to a single held-out score, but that score can conflate qualitatively different failures. We study a controlled minimal GPT trained on exhaustive 2-digit addition, where all local digit transitions are already present in training, and ask why 3-digit generalization still fails. The failure is staged. First, there is a layout barrier: a learned absolute-position model collapses under a pure 3-digit layout shift, and mixed-layout exposure is the only intervention that materially weakens this barrier. Second, after layout repair, the hundreds position behaves like a carry flag rather than a semantic hundreds digit; targeted carry probes reverse the relevant logit margin, whereas a matched extra-data control does not. Third, after carry repair, the main remaining bottleneck is conditional recomposition: high-conditioned tail data outperforms a matched control, high-only data, and tail-only data on all true-3-digit suites, and the same ordering reappears in a larger 2-layer bridge experiment. The residual errors after recomposition are then overwhelmingly tens-only, and a separate 10-seed late-stage study shows that a sign-aware tens repair raises exact match on the hardest thousands-carry suite from 0.664 to 0.822. We therefore provide an experimentally testable decomposition of arithmetic OOD failure into layout, carry-semantics, recomposition, and late tens-residual stages.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates why GPT-based models trained for exhaustive 2-digit addition fail to generalize to 3-digit addition, identifying specific barriers at different stages of the generalization process.
AI.motivation: To understand the limitations of arithmetic generalization in GPT models—particularly why generalization from 2-digit addition to 3-digit addition fails despite exhaustive 2-digit addition training.
AI.method: The study uses a controlled minimal GPT model to identify barriers to generalization. Through staged experiments, they analyze: 1) Layout issues (absolute-position vs. mixed-layout exposure), 2) Carry-semantics malfunction (matching logits and carry probes), and 3) Conditional recomposition and residual errors in high-conditioned data.
AI.result: The authors identify distinct failure stages: layout collapse, semantic errors in the hundreds position acting as a carry flag, and recomposition barriers. After fixing these issues, residual tens-position errors emerged, with targeted repair improving accuracy significantly (e.g., on hardest thousands-carry suite from 0.664 to 0.822).
AI.conclusion: Arithmetic out-of-distribution (OOD) failures in GPT models can be experimentally decomposed into specific stages: layout shift, carry semantic errors, recompositional bottlenecks, and residual tens-position errors. Addressing these stages sequentially allows for improved generalization in tasks like 3-digit addition.

title: Surface-Constrained Offline Warping with Contact-Aware Online Pose Projection for Safe Robotic Trajectory Execution
summary: Robotic manipulation tasks that require repeated tool motion along curved surfaces frequently arise in surface finishing, inspection, and guided interaction. In practice, nominal motion primitives are often designed independently of the deployment surface and later reused across varying geometries. Directly tiling such primitives onto nonplanar surfaces introduces geometric inconsistencies, leading to interpenetration, orientation discontinuities, and cumulative drift over repeated cycles. We present a two-stage framework that separates geometric embedding from execution-level regulation. An offline surface-constrained warping operator embeds a nominal periodic primitive onto curved surfaces through asymmetric diffeomorphic deformation of dual-track waypoints and axis-consistent orientation completion, producing a surface-adapted reference trajectory. An online contact-aware projection operator then enforces bounded deviation relative to this reference using FSR-driven disturbance adaptation and a conic orientation safety constraint. Experiments across multiple analytic surface families and real-robot validation on a sinusoidal surface demonstrate improved geometric continuity, reduced large orientation jumps, and robust contact maintenance compared with direct tiling. These results show that decoupling offline geometric remapping from lightweight online projection enables stable and repeatable surface-embedded trajectory execution under sensor-lite feedbacks.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses geometric inconsistencies when applying nominal motion primitives to curved surfaces, presenting a two-stage framework to improve trajectory execution in robotic manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: Robotic manipulation tasks often encounter issues when nominal motion primitives designed independently of surface geometry cause problems like drift, interpenetration, and orientation discontinuities on nonplanar surfaces.
AI.method: The framework consists of two stages: 1) Offline surface-constrained warping operator that adapts motion primitives for curved surfaces through deformation techniques. 2) Online contact-aware projection operator that ensures trajectory execution remains accurate with disturbance adaptation and orientation constraints.
AI.result: Experiments on various surface geometries and real-robot validations demonstrated improved geometric continuity, minimized orientation jumps, and effective contact maintenance compared to direct tiling methods.
AI.conclusion: Separating the processes of offline trajectory adaptation and lightweight online regulation yields stable and repeatable execution of surface-embedded trajectories, even with minimal sensor feedback.

title: Predicting Program Correctness By Ensemble Semantic Entropy
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in generating programs from natural language descriptions, yet ensuring their correctness without an external oracle remains a critical challenge. To solve the challenge, existing methods often rely on uncertainty estimation, measuring the consistency of semantics or execution behaviors across multiple samples generated by a single model. However, we observe that a single model can often converge to a consistent but incorrect solution, rendering such consistency-based proxies ineffective. To address this, we propose Ensemble Semantic Entropy (ESE), which estimates uncertainty by evaluating the consistency of samples aggregated across an ensemble of models. Experiments on LiveCodeBench demonstrate that ESE correlates more strongly with program correctness than single-model semantic entropy. Notably, in selective generation tasks with strict false-positive rate constraints, ESE improves prediction accuracy by 53.4%. Furthermore, by leveraging ESE as the decision signal, we propose a cascading test-time scaling framework Cas, which maintains performance while reducing FLOPs by 64.9% compared to single-model scaling, offering a new perspective on balancing parameter and inference scaling.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Ensemble Semantic Entropy (ESE), which uses multiple models to improve program correctness estimation and proposes Cascading Framework (Cas) to enhance efficiency.
AI.motivation: Ensuring the correctness of large language model (LLM)-generated programs remains challenging. The limitation of single-model uncertainty estimation motivated the need for a more reliable approach.
AI.method: The paper introduces ESE to aggregate semantic consistency across multiple models for uncertainty measurement and proposes Cas, a framework using ESE to optimize inference scaling.
AI.result: On LiveCodeBench dataset, ESE showed stronger correlation with correctness, improving prediction accuracy by 53.4% in selective tasks. Cas reduced computational cost by 64.9% while maintaining performance.
AI.conclusion: ESE enhances reliability in correctness prediction, and Cas demonstrates improved efficiency, balancing accuracy and computational cost.

title: MemGuard-Alpha: Detecting and Filtering Memorization-Contaminated Signals in LLM-Based Financial Forecasting via Membership Inference and Cross-Model Disagreement
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate financial alpha signals, yet growing evidence shows that LLMs memorize historical financial data from their training corpora, producing spurious predictive accuracy that collapses out-of-sample. This memorization-induced look-ahead bias threatens the validity of LLM-based quantitative strategies. Prior remedies -- model retraining and input anonymization -- are either prohibitively expensive or introduce significant information loss. No existing method offers practical, zero-cost signal-level filtering for real-time trading. We introduce MemGuard-Alpha, a post-generation framework comprising two algorithms: (i) the MemGuard Composite Score (MCS), which combines five membership inference attack (MIA) methods with temporal proximity features via logistic regression, achieving Cohen's d = 18.57 for contamination separation (d = 0.39-1.37 using MIA features alone); and (ii) Cross-Model Memorization Disagreement (CMMD), which exploits variation in training cutoff dates across LLMs to separate memorized signals from genuine reasoning. Evaluated across seven LLMs (124M-7B parameters), 50 S&P 100 stocks, 42,800 prompts, and five MIA methods over 5.5 years (2019-2024), CMMD achieves a Sharpe ratio of 4.11 versus 2.76 for unfiltered signals (49% improvement). Clean signals produce 14.48 bps average daily return versus 2.13 bps for tainted signals (7x difference). A striking crossover pattern emerges: in-sample accuracy rises with contamination (40.8% to 52.5%) while out-of-sample accuracy falls (47% to 42%), providing direct evidence that memorization inflates apparent accuracy at the cost of generalization.

AI.tldr: MemGuard-Alpha introduces novel algorithms to detect and correct memorization-induced look-ahead bias in large language models (LLMs) used for financial alpha signal generation, improving both return and reliability of predictions.
AI.motivation: The study addresses the issue of memorization-induced bias in LLMs, which leads to spurious accuracy and unreliable predictions in financial modeling strategies.
AI.method: Two algorithms are proposed: MemGuard Composite Score (MCS), which combines multiple membership inference attack methods for contamination separation, and Cross-Model Memorization Disagreement (CMMD), which identifies memorized signals by leveraging varying training cutoff dates across LLMs.
AI.result: MemGuard-Alpha significantly improves financial signal reliability, achieving a 49% Sharpe ratio increase and producing 7x higher average daily returns for clean data compared to tainted data.
AI.conclusion: MemGuard-Alpha effectively mitigates bias without retraining or data anonymization, offering a practical solution for real-time financial trading using LLMs.

title: Ordinal Semantic Segmentation Applied to Medical and Odontological Images
summary: Semantic segmentation consists of assigning a semantic label to each pixel according to predefined classes. This process facilitates the understanding of object appearance and spatial relationships, playing an important role in the global interpretation of image content. Although modern deep learning approaches achieve high accuracy, they often ignore ordinal relationships among classes, which may encode important domain knowledge for scene interpretation. In this work, loss functions that incorporate ordinal relationships into deep neural networks are investigated to promote greater semantic consistency in semantic segmentation tasks. These loss functions are categorized as unimodal, quasi-unimodal, and spatial. Unimodal losses constrain the predicted probability distribution according to the class ordering, while quasi-unimodal losses relax this constraint by allowing small variations while preserving ordinal coherence. Spatial losses penalize semantic inconsistencies between neighboring pixels, encouraging smoother transitions in the image space. In particular, this study adapts loss functions originally proposed for ordinal classification to ordinal semantic segmentation. Among them, the Expanded Mean Squared Error (EXP_MSE), the Quasi-Unimodal Loss (QUL), and the spatial Contact Surface Loss using Signal Distance Function (CSSDF) are investigated. These approaches have shown promising results in medical imaging, improving robustness, generalization, and anatomical consistency.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses semantic segmentation and proposes loss functions to incorporate ordinal relationships into deep neural networks.
AI.motivation: Standard deep learning methods for semantic segmentation neglect ordinal relationships among classes, which are vital for scene interpretation and global image understanding.
AI.method: Investigating and adapting loss functions such as Expanded Mean Squared Error (EXP_MSE), Quasi-Unimodal Loss (QUL), and Contact Surface Loss using Signal Distance Function (CSSDF) to improve semantic consistency.
AI.result: Proposed loss functions demonstrate promising outcomes in medical imaging, improving robustness, generalization, and anatomical accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating ordinal relationships into loss functions enhances semantic segmentation by promoting greater semantic consistency and smoother class transitions, especially in medical applications.

title: RDEx-MOP: Indicator-Guided Reconstructed Differential Evolution for Fixed-Budget Multiobjective Optimization
summary: Multiobjective optimisation in the CEC 2025 MOP track is evaluated not only by final IGD values but also by how quickly an algorithm reaches the target region under a fixed evaluation budget. This report documents RDEx-MOP, the reconstructed differential evolution variant used in the IEEE CEC 2025 numerical optimisation competition (C06 special session) bound-constrained multiobjective track. RDEx-MOP integrates indicator-based environmental selection, a niche-maintained Pareto-candidate set, and complementary differential evolution operators for exploration and exploitation. We evaluate RDEx-MOP on the official CEC 2025 MOP benchmark using the released checkpoint traces and the median-target U-score framework. Experimental results show that RDEx-MOP achieves the highest total score and the best average rank among all released comparison algorithms, including the earlier RDEx baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper discusses RDEx-MOP, a multiobjective optimization method shown to outperform others in the CEC 2025 competition using various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve multiobjective optimization under constrained evaluation budgets, with a focus on early convergence to the target regions.
AI.method: RDEx-MOP integrates indicator-based selection, niche-maintained Pareto-candidate sets, and differential evolution operators for both exploration and exploitation purposes.
AI.result: RDEx-MOP achieved the highest score and best average rank among comparison algorithms in the CEC 2025 MOP benchmark.
AI.conclusion: RDEx-MOP demonstrates superior performance in multiobjective optimization benchmarks, succeeding in both efficiency and effectiveness.

title: jaxsgp4: GPU-accelerated mega-constellation propagation with batch parallelism
summary: As the population of anthropogenic space objects transitions from sparse clusters to mega-constellations exceeding 100,000 satellites, traditional orbital propagation techniques face a critical bottleneck. Standard CPU-bound implementations of the Simplified General Perturbations 4 (SGP4) algorithm are less well suited to handle the requisite scale of collision avoidance and Space Situational Awareness (SSA) tasks. This paper introduces \texttt{jaxsgp4}, an open-source high-performance reimplementation of SGP4 utilising the \texttt{JAX} library. \texttt{JAX} has gained traction in the landscape of computational research, offering an easy mechanism for Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation, automatic vectorisation and automatic optimisation of code for CPU, GPU and TPU hardware modalities. By refactoring the algorithm into a pure functional paradigm, we leverage these transformations to execute massively parallel propagations on modern GPUs. We demonstrate that \texttt{jaxsgp4} can propagate the entire Starlink constellation (9,341 satellites) each to 1,000 future time steps in under 4 ms on a single A100 GPU, representing a speedup of $1500\times$ over traditional C++ baselines. Furthermore, we argue that the use of 32-bit precision for SGP4 propagation tasks offers a principled trade-off, sacrificing negligible precision loss for a substantial gain in throughput on hardware accelerators.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces 'jaxsgp4', a high-performance reimplementation of the SGP4 algorithm, achieving massive speedups leveraging JAX and GPU acceleration, aimed at modern space collision avoidance tasks.
AI.motivation: The rapid growth in space objects, particularly mega-constellations, demands a scalable solution for collision avoidance and Space Situational Awareness tasks, which traditional SGP4 implementations cannot handle efficiently.
AI.method: The authors reimplement the SGP4 algorithm using the JAX library, transforming it into a pure functional paradigm. This enables Just-In-Time compilation, vectorisation, and optimisation for hardware accelerators like GPUs.
AI.result: The 'jaxsgp4' implementation achieves a significant speedup, propagating 9,341 satellites with 1,000 time steps in under 4 ms on a single A100 GPU—a 1500x improvement over conventional C++ methods.
AI.conclusion: The paper highlights the benefits of leveraging modern computational frameworks and hardware for scalable satellite trajectory prediction, demonstrating minimal precision loss while achieving significant performance gains.

title: Energy Score-Guided Neural Gaussian Mixture Model for Predictive Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Quantifying predictive uncertainty is essential for real world machine learning applications, especially in scenarios requiring reliable and interpretable predictions. Many common parametric approaches rely on neural networks to estimate distribution parameters by optimizing the negative log likelihood. However, these methods often encounter challenges like training instability and mode collapse, leading to poor estimates of the mean and variance of the target output distribution. In this work, we propose the Neural Energy Gaussian Mixture Model (NE-GMM), a novel framework that integrates Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) with Energy Score (ES) to enhance predictive uncertainty quantification. NE-GMM leverages the flexibility of GMM to capture complex multimodal distributions and leverages the robustness of ES to ensure well calibrated predictions in diverse scenarios. We theoretically prove that the hybrid loss function satisfies the properties of a strictly proper scoring rule, ensuring alignment with the true data distribution, and establish generalization error bounds, demonstrating that the model's empirical performance closely aligns with its expected performance on unseen data. Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real world datasets demonstrate the superiority of NE-GMM in terms of both predictive accuracy and uncertainty quantification.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NE-GMM, combining Gaussian Mixture Models and Energy Score for better predictive uncertainty and calibration, supported by theoretical and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: Reliable predictive uncertainty quantification is crucial but often hampered by issues like mode collapse and training instability in neural networks.
AI.method: Proposed NE-GMM, combining GMM's flexibility for multimodal distributions and ES's robustness for calibration, with a hybrid loss function proven to align with true data distributions.
AI.result: NE-GMM showcases improved accuracy and uncertainty quantification in experiments on synthetic and real-world datasets.
AI.conclusion: The NE-GMM framework effectively addresses predictive uncertainty challenges, offering robust and interpretable predictions with strong theoretical underpinnings and empirical success.

title: Persistent Memory Through Triple-Loop Consolidation in a Non-Gradient Dissipative Cognitive Architecture
comment: 28 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables. Submitted to Frontiers in Computational Neuroscience. Ancillary file: dm_minimal_reproduction.py (NumPy-only reproduction script, ~200 lines)
summary:
Dissipative cognitive architectures maintain computation through continuous energy expenditure, where units that exhaust their energy are stochastically replaced with fresh random state. This creates a fundamental challenge: how can persistent, context-specific memory survive when all learnable state is periodically destroyed? Existing memory mechanisms -- including elastic weight consolidation, synaptic intelligence, and surprise-driven gating -- rely on gradient computation and are inapplicable to non-gradient dissipative systems.
  We introduce Deep Memory (DM), a non-gradient persistent memory mechanism operating through a triple-loop consolidation cycle: (1) recording of expert-specific content centroids, (2) seeding of replaced units with stored representations, and (3) stabilization through continuous re-entry. We demonstrate that discrete expert routing via Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) gating is a causal prerequisite for DM, preventing centroid convergence that would render stored memories identical.
  Across ${\sim}970$ simulation runs spanning thirteen experimental blocks: (i) discrete routing is causally necessary for specialization ($\text{MI}=1.10$ vs. $0.001$; $n=91$); (ii) DM achieves $R=0.984$ vs. $0.385$ without memory ($n=16$); (iii) continuous seeding reconstructs representations after interference ($R_\mathrm{recon}=0.978$; one-shot fails; $n=30$); (iv) the mechanism operates within a characterized $(K,p)$ envelope ($n=350$); (v) recording $\times$ seeding is the minimal critical dyad ($n=40$); (vi) DM outperforms non-gradient baselines (Hopfield, ESN) under matched turnover ($n=370$). These results establish DM as a falsifiable mechanism for persistent memory in non-gradient cognitive systems, with functional parallels to hippocampal consolidation.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Deep Memory (DM), a non-gradient persistent memory mechanism for dissipative cognitive architectures, addressing challenges of maintaining memory in systems with frequent state replacement.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of preserving persistent, context-specific memory in dissipative cognitive systems, where units undergo periodic state destruction.
AI.method: Deep Memory (DM) is introduced, featuring a triple-loop consolidation system comprising: (1) recording expert-specific centroids, (2) seeding replaced units with stored representations, and (3) continuous stabilization via re-entry.
AI.result: Key findings include DM's necessity for discrete expert routing, superior reconstruction after interference, operation within specific parameter envelopes, and performance surpassing non-gradient baselines.
AI.conclusion: Deep Memory serves as a falsifiable mechanism for persistent memory in non-gradient systems, with analogies to hippocampal consolidation in biological systems.

title: Magic Words or Methodical Work? Challenging Conventional Wisdom in LLM-Based Political Text Annotation
summary: Political scientists are rapidly adopting large language models (LLMs) for text annotation, yet the sensitivity of annotation results to implementation choices remains poorly understood. Most evaluations test a single model or configuration; how model choice, model size, learning approach, and prompt style interact, and whether popular "best practices" survive controlled comparison, are largely unexplored. We present a controlled evaluation of these pipeline choices, testing six open-weight models across four political science annotation tasks under identical quantisation, hardware, and prompt-template conditions. Our central finding is methodological: interaction effects dominate main effects, so seemingly reasonable pipeline choices can become consequential researcher degrees of freedom. No single model, prompt style, or learning approach is uniformly superior, and the best-performing model varies across tasks. Two corollaries follow. First, model size is an unreliable guide both to cost and to performance: cross-family efficiency differences are so large that some larger models are less resource-intensive than much smaller alternatives, while within model families mid-range variants often match or exceed larger counterparts. Second, widely recommended prompt engineering techniques yield inconsistent and sometimes negative effects on annotation performance. We use these benchmark results to develop a validation-first framework - with a principled ordering of pipeline decisions, guidance on prompt freezing and held-out evaluation, reporting standards, and open-source tools - to help researchers navigate this decision space transparently.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates how choices in large language model pipelines significantly impact text annotation outcomes in political science tasks, finding unpredictable interactions and offering a framework to guide researchers.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to identify how various implementation decisions in using LLMs for text annotation may introduce researcher degrees of freedom and impact results, which is poorly understood.
AI.method: The authors conducted controlled evaluations with six open-weight models across four annotation tasks, standardizing quantization, hardware, and prompt styles, and compared effects of pipeline choices.
AI.result: Interaction effects influenced outcomes significantly, with no single model, size, or prompt style consistently superior. Larger models aren’t always resource-intensive, and prompt engineering sometimes hinders performance.
AI.conclusion: A validation-first framework was proposed to transparently navigate pipeline decisions, including guidance on model selection, prompt usage, evaluation transparency, and reporting tools.

title: SutureAgent: Learning Surgical Trajectories via Goal-conditioned Offline RL in Pixel Space
summary: Predicting surgical needle trajectories from endoscopic video is critical for robot-assisted suturing, enabling anticipatory planning, real-time guidance, and safer motion execution. Existing methods that directly learn motion distributions from visual observations tend to overlook the sequential dependency among adjacent motion steps. Moreover, sparse waypoint annotations often fail to provide sufficient supervision, further increasing the difficulty of supervised or imitation learning methods. To address these challenges, we formulate image-based needle trajectory prediction as a sequential decision-making problem, in which the needle tip is treated as an agent that moves step by step in pixel space. This formulation naturally captures the continuity of needle motion and enables the explicit modeling of physically plausible pixel-wise state transitions over time. From this perspective, we propose SutureAgent, a goal-conditioned offline reinforcement learning framework that leverages sparse annotations to dense reward signals via cubic spline interpolation, encouraging the policy to exploit limited expert guidance while exploring plausible future motion paths.
