To examine our proposed neural-aided adaptive invariant Kalman filter, we focus on the challenging real-world scenario of autonomous underwater navigation. Experimental results demonstrate superior performance compared to existing methods in terms of position root mean square error. These results validate our sim2real pipeline and further confirm that geometric invariance significantly enhances learning-based adaptation and that adaptive noise estimation in the tangent Lie space offers a powerful mechanism for improving navigation accuracy in nonlinear autonomous platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel approach combining invariant Kalman filtering and neural-aided noise adaptation in the tangent Lie space for improved autonomous platform positioning, particularly for underwater navigation.
AI.motivation: Accurate positioning is critical for autonomous systems, but traditional adaptation techniques are underexplored in tangent Lie space, which offers advantages for nonlinear systems. The study aims to address this gap.
AI.method: The authors develop a theoretical extension of noise adaptation within the Lie group framework and design a lightweight neural network to estimate process noise covariance from inertial data. The network is trained with a sim2real domain adaptation.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates superior performance in real-world autonomous underwater navigation, achieving better position root mean square error compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The study validates the proposed pipeline, showcasing the benefits of combining geometric invariance with neural-aided adaptation in challenging navigation tasks.

title: KVSculpt: KV Cache Compression as Distillation
summary: KV cache compression is critical for efficient long-context LLM inference. Approaches that reduce the per-pair footprint -- quantization and low-rank decomposition -- are orthogonal to those that reduce the sequence length of the cache. Along the sequence-length dimension, existing methods range from pure eviction -- selecting which KV pairs to keep -- to merging, which combines similar pairs into fewer ones. Both remain anchored to the original cache entries. We propose KVSculpt, which moves to the other end of this spectrum: instead of selecting or combining original pairs, we optimize a smaller set of unconstrained KV pairs in continuous embedding space to preserve each layer's attention behavior. Keys are optimized via L-BFGS and values are solved in closed form via least squares, alternating every few steps. On top of this, we introduce adaptive budget allocation, which uses a cheap pilot compression run to redistribute the compression budget across layers and KV heads based on per-component difficulty. On Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct with 2048-token contexts, KVSculpt reduces KL divergence by 3.5-4.1x compared to Select+Fit -- attention-score eviction with least-squares value fitting -- across compression ratios r in {0.3, 0.5, 0.7}. Adaptive allocation provides an additional 1.3x KL reduction at no extra inference cost. Analysis reveals that compression difficulty is highly non-uniform: per-layer pilot MSE varies by up to 100x across layers, and the two KV heads within a single layer can differ by up to 467x -- demonstrating that fine-grained budget allocation is essential.

AI.tldr: KVSculpt introduces a new approach to optimizing KV cache compression for large language models by creating a smaller set of unconstrained KV pairs instead of using traditional selection or merging methods. It achieves improved attention behavior preservation and outperforms existing techniques in minimizing KL divergence.
AI.motivation: Efficient long-context language model (LLM) inference requires reducing the overhead of KV cache. Current methods, while useful, remain dependent on original cache entries, which hinders maximal compression efficiency and adaptability. The goal is to develop a more flexible and effective technique.
AI.method: KVSculpt optimizes a reduced set of unconstrained KV pairs directly in the continuous embedding space. It uses L-BFGS optimization for keys and a closed-form least-squares solution for values, alternating between them. Additionally, adaptive budget allocation redistributes resources across layers and KV heads based on their specific compression difficulty, guided by a pilot compression run.
AI.result: KVSculpt achieves a 3.5-4.1x reduction in KL divergence compared to existing methods like Select+Fit at various compression ratios, and its adaptive budget allocation adds an extra 1.3x reduction without increasing inference cost. Analysis highlights significant variation in compression difficulty across layers and KV heads.
AI.conclusion: KVSculpt demonstrates a substantial advancement in KV cache compression by moving away from traditional selection/merging strategies. Adaptive budget allocation further enhances performance, highlighting the necessity of fine-grained resource distribution for efficient compression. The method shows potential for more efficient LLM inference without compromising model accuracy.

title: RiskProp: Collision-Anchored Self-Supervised Risk Propagation for Early Accident Anticipation
summary: Accident anticipation aims to predict impending collisions from dashcam videos and trigger early alerts. Existing methods rely on binary supervision with manually annotated "anomaly onset" frames, which are subjective and inconsistent, leading to inaccurate risk estimation. In contrast, we propose RiskProp, a novel collision-anchored self-supervised risk propagation paradigm for early accident anticipation, which removes the need for anomaly onset annotations and leverages only the reliably annotated collision frame. RiskProp models temporal risk evolution through two observation-driven losses: first, since future frames contain more definitive evidence of an impending accident, we introduce a future-frame regularization loss that uses the model's next-frame prediction as a soft target to supervise the current frame, enabling backward propagation of risk signals; second, inspired by the empirical trend of rising risk before accidents, we design an adaptive monotonic constraint to encourage a non-decreasing progression over time. Experiments on CAP and Nexar demonstrate that RiskProp achieves state-of-the-art performance and produces smoother, more discriminative risk curves, improving both early anticipation and interpretability.

AI.tldr: RiskProp introduces a self-supervised system for accident anticipation that avoids subjective manual annotations and achieves state-of-the-art performance by focusing on reliably annotated collision frames.
AI.motivation: Current accident anticipation models rely on subjective and inconsistent annotations, leading to poor risk prediction.
AI.method: RiskProp uses a self-supervised paradigm based on collision frames and introduces two losses: future-frame regularization and an adaptive monotonic constraint, enabling better risk modeling.
AI.result: Experiments on CAP and Nexar datasets show state-of-the-art results with smoother and more interpretable risk predictions.
AI.conclusion: RiskProp provides a more reliable and interpretable approach to early accident anticipation, avoiding reliance on subjective annotations.

title: Stability and Sensitivity Analysis of Relative Temporal-Difference Learning: Extended Version
comment: Extended version for submission to the 2026 IEEE CDC
summary: Relative temporal-difference (TD) learning was introduced to mitigate the slow convergence of TD methods when the discount factor approaches one by subtracting a baseline from the temporal-difference update. While this idea has been studied in the tabular setting, stability guarantees with function approximation remain poorly understood. This paper analyzes relative TD learning with linear function approximation. We establish stability conditions for the algorithm and show that the choice of baseline distribution plays a central role. In particular, when the baseline is chosen as the empirical distribution of the state-action process, the algorithm is stable for any non-negative baseline weight and any discount factor. We also provide a sensitivity analysis of the resulting parameter estimates, characterizing both asymptotic bias and covariance. The asymptotic covariance and asymptotic bias are shown to remain uniformly bounded as the discount factor approaches one.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes relative temporal-difference (TD) learning with linear function approximation, focusing on stability conditions and baseline influences, demonstrating the algorithm's stability under certain conditions.
AI.motivation: To address the slow convergence of temporal-difference (TD) learning methods, especially when the discount factor nears one, and to clarify stability guarantees with function approximation.
AI.method: The authors study relative TD learning in the context of linear function approximation, examining stability conditions under different choices of baseline distributions and analyzing parameter sensitivity.
AI.result: The paper establishes that the choice of baseline distribution is crucial for algorithm stability, particularly showing stability for empirical baseline distributions, irrespective of baseline weight or discount factor.
AI.conclusion: Relative TD learning can achieve stability and bounded bias/covariance under proper baseline choices, even when the discount factor approaches one, emphasizing the importance of the baseline selection.

title: MultiLoc: Multi-view Guided Relative Pose Regression for Fast and Robust Visual Re-Localization
summary: Relative Pose Regression (RPR) generalizes well to unseen environments, but its performance is often limited due to pairwise and local spatial views. To this end, we propose MultiLoc, a novel multi-view guided RPR model trained at scale, equipping relative pose regression with globally consistent spatial and geometric understanding. Specifically, our method jointly fuses multiple reference views and their associated camera poses in a single forward pass, enabling accurate zero-shot pose estimation with real-time efficiency. To reliably supply informative context, we further propose a co-visibility-driven retrieval strategy for geometrically relevant reference view selection. MultiLoc establishes a new benchmark in visual re-localization, consistently outperforming existing state-of-the-art (SOTA) relative pose regression (RPR) methods across diverse datasets, including WaySpots, Cambridge Landmarks, and Indoor6. Furthermore, MultiLoc's pose regressor exhibits SOTA performance in relative pose estimation, surpassing RPR, feature matching and non-regression-based techniques on the MegaDepth-1500, ScanNet-1500, and ACID benchmarks. These results demonstrate robust domain generalization of MultiLoc across indoor, outdoor and natural environments. Code will be made publicly available.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces MultiLoc, a multi-view guided relative pose regression model that ensures globally consistent spatial understanding, achieving state-of-the-art performance across various datasets and environments.
AI.motivation: Current RPR models are limited by their reliance on pairwise and local spatial views, which hampers their performance. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by introducing a model that incorporates global spatial and geometric context.
AI.method: The proposed MultiLoc model jointly fuses multiple reference views and their camera poses in a single forward pass. It utilizes a co-visibility-driven retrieval strategy to select geometrically relevant reference views, enhancing the model's spatial understanding.
AI.result: MultiLoc outperforms existing RPR methods on datasets like WaySpots, Cambridge Landmarks, and Indoor6. It also surpasses RPR, feature matching, and non-regression methods in relative pose estimation on benchmarks such as MegaDepth-1500, ScanNet-1500, and ACID.
AI.conclusion: MultiLoc demonstrates robust domain generalization and establishes itself as a new benchmark for visual re-localization across diverse environments by addressing the limitations of traditional RPR approaches.

title: Agentic AI for Human Resources: LLM-Driven Candidate Assessment
comment: Published in 19th Conference of the European Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics (EACL 2026)
summary:
In this work, we present a modular and interpretable framework that uses Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate candidate assessment in recruitment. The system integrates diverse sources, including job descriptions, CVs, interview transcripts, and HR feedback; to generate structured evaluation reports that mirror expert judgment. Unlike traditional ATS tools that rely on keyword matching or shallow scoring, our approach employs role-specific, LLM-generated rubrics and a multi-agent architecture to perform fine-grained, criteria-driven evaluations. The framework outputs detailed assessment reports, candidate comparisons, and ranked recommendations that are transparent, auditable, and suitable for real-world hiring workflows.
  Beyond rubric-based analysis, we introduce an LLM-Driven Active Listwise Tournament mechanism for candidate ranking. Instead of noisy pairwise comparisons or inconsistent independent scoring, the LLM ranks small candidate subsets (mini-tournaments), and these listwise permutations are aggregated using a Plackett-Luce model. An active-learning loop selects the most informative subsets, producing globally coherent and sample-efficient rankings. This adaptation of listwise LLM preference modeling (previously explored in financial asset ranking) provides a principled and highly interpretable methodology for large-scale candidate ranking in talent acquisition.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a modular, interpretable framework leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) to automate and enhance recruitment candidate assessments, integrating diverse data sources and employing innovative ranking mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of traditional recruitment tools that rely on keyword matching and shallow scoring by introducing a robust, expert-like candidate assessment and ranking system.
AI.method: Developed a system that uses LLMs to create role-specific rubrics for structured evaluations and employs a multi-agent architecture. Introduced an LLM-Driven Active Listwise Tournament for ranking, supported by a Plackett-Luce model and active-learning loop.
AI.result: The proposed framework generates detailed assessment reports, transparent rankings, and globally coherent candidate recommendations, demonstrating suitability for real-world hiring workflows.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a principled, sample-efficient, and interpretable solution for automating candidate assessment and ranking, setting a new standard in talent acquisition technology.

title: Kernel Dynamics under Path Entropy Maximization
summary: We propose a variational framework in which the kernel function k : X x X -> R, interpreted as the foundational object encoding what distinctions an agent can represent, is treated as a dynamical variable subject to path entropy maximization (Maximum Caliber, MaxCal). Each kernel defines a representational structure over which an information geometry on probability space may be analyzed; a trajectory through kernel space therefore corresponds to a trajectory through a family of effective geometries, making the optimization landscape endogenous to its own traversal. We formulate fixed-point conditions for self-consistent kernels, propose renormalization group (RG) flow as a structured special case, and suggest neural tangent kernel (NTK) evolution during deep network training as a candidate empirical instantiation. Under explicit information-thermodynamic assumptions, the work required for kernel change is bounded below by delta W >= k_B T delta I_k, where delta I_k is the mutual information newly unlocked by the updated kernel. In this view, stable fixed points of MaxCal over kernels correspond to self-reinforcing distinction structures, with biological niches, scientific paradigms, and craft mastery offered as conjectural interpretations. We situate the framework relative to assembly theory and the MaxCal literature, separate formal results from structured correspondences and conjectural bridges, and pose six open questions that make the program empirically and mathematically testable.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a variational framework that optimizes the kernel function, which encodes representational distinctions, as a dynamical variable through path entropy maximization. It explores self-consistent kernels, renormalization flow, associated thermodynamic costs, and empirical modeling via neural networks.
AI.motivation: This paper aims to develop a kernel-based framework to analyze representational structures that change dynamically, inspired by principles from information geometry, Maximum Caliber, and empirical phenomena such as neural network evolution.
AI.method: The authors utilize a variational approach where kernels evolve through path entropy maximization. They examine fixed-point conditions, renormalization group flows as structured instances, and thermodynamic bounds tied to mutual information.
AI.result: The paper establishes theoretical constructs like self-consistent kernels, thermodynamic bounds on informational changes, and draws connections to empirical systems like NTK evolution in deep networks.
AI.conclusion: The framework ties kernel optimization to evolving representational structures, proposing stable fixed points as significant entities in diverse contexts like biology or science paradigms, and outlines open mathematical and empirical questions.

title: MEDIC-AD: Towards Medical Vision-Language Model's Clinical Intelligence
summary: Lesion detection, symptom tracking, and visual explainability are central to real-world medical image analysis, yet current medical Vision-Language Models (VLMs) still lack mechanisms that translate their broad knowledge into clinically actionable outputs. To bridge this gap, we present MEDIC-AD, a clinically oriented VLM that strengthens these three capabilities through a stage-wise framework. First, learnable anomaly-aware tokens (<Ano>) encourage the model to focus on abnormal regions and build more discriminative lesion centered representations. Second, inter image difference tokens (<Diff>) explicitly encode temporal changes between studies, allowing the model to distinguish worsening, improvement, and stability in disease burden. Finally, a dedicated explainability stage trains the model to generate heatmaps that highlight lesion-related regions, offering clear visual evidence that is consistent with the model's reasoning. Through our staged design, MEDIC-AD steadily boosts performance across anomaly detection, symptom tracking, and anomaly segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art results compared with both closed source and medical-specialized baselines. Evaluations on real longitudinal clinical data collected from real hospital workflows further show that MEDIC-AD delivers stable predictions and clinically faithful explanations in practical patient-monitoring and decision-support workflows

AI.tldr: MEDIC-AD is a Vision-Language Model (VLM) designed to enhance lesion detection, symptom tracking, and visual explainability in medical image analysis. It achieves state-of-the-art performance using a stage-wise framework.
AI.motivation: Existing medical VLMs lack the ability to provide actionable clinical outputs, such as accurate lesion detection, disease progression tracking, and visual explanations, which are critical in real clinical applications.
AI.method: The paper introduces MEDIC-AD, which uses a stage-wise framework with three novel mechanisms: (1) anomaly-aware tokens to focus on abnormal regions, (2) difference tokens for encoding temporal changes, and (3) an explainability stage for generating heatmaps of lesions.
AI.result: MEDIC-AD demonstrates state-of-the-art performance across anomaly detection, tracking, and segmentation tasks, outperforming existing baselines. It is also validated on real hospital data, showing robust and clinically faithful predictions.
AI.conclusion: MEDIC-AD effectively bridges the gap between general medical VLMs and clinically actionable tools. It enhances lesion detection, tracking, and explainability, making it suitable for real-world clinical workflows.

title: SEAR: Schema-Based Evaluation and Routing for LLM Gateways
summary: Evaluating production LLM responses and routing requests across providers in LLM gateways requires fine-grained quality signals and operationally grounded decisions. To address this gap, we present SEAR, a schema-based evaluation and routing system for multi-model, multi-provider LLM gateways. SEAR defines an extensible relational schema covering both LLM evaluation signals (context, intent, response characteristics, issue attribution, and quality scores) and gateway operational metrics (latency, cost, throughput), with cross-table consistency links across around one hundred typed, SQL-queryable columns. To populate the evaluation signals reliably, SEAR proposes self-contained signal instructions, in-schema reasoning, and multi-stage generation that produces database-ready structured outputs. Because signals are derived through LLM reasoning rather than shallow classifiers, SEAR captures complex request semantics, enables human-interpretable routing explanations, and unifies evaluation and routing in a single query layer. Across thousands of production sessions, SEAR achieves strong signal accuracy on human-labeled data and supports practical routing decisions, including large cost reductions with comparable quality.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SEAR, a schema-based evaluation and routing system for multi-model, multi-provider LLM gateways to enhance quality and operational decisions.
AI.motivation: The motivation is the need for fine-grained quality signals and operationally sound decision-making in evaluating production LLM responses and optimizing request routing across various LLM providers.
AI.method: The method employs the SEAR system, which uses an extensible relational schema and proposes self-contained signal instructions, in-schema reasoning, and multi-stage generation for structured outputs.
AI.result: SEAR demonstrated high signal accuracy and enabled effective routing decisions with significant cost savings and comparable quality across thousands of production sessions.
AI.conclusion: SEAR effectively unifies evaluation and routing in a single query layer, capturing complex semantics and offering interpretable explanations for decision-making, showing promise for practical LLM gateway optimization.

title: Degrees, Levels, and Profiles of Contextuality
summary: We introduce a new notion, that of a contextuality profile of a system. Rather than characterizing a system's contextuality by a single number, its overall degree of contextuality, we show how it can be characterized by a curve relating degree of contextuality to level at which the system is considered,\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c} \textnormal{level} & 1 & \cdots & n-1 & n>1 & n+1 & \cdots & N\\ \hline \textnormal{degree} & 0 & \cdots & 0 & d_{n}>0 & d_{n+1}\geq d_{n} & \cdots & d_{N}\geq d_{N-1} \end{array},where N is the maximum number of variables per system's context. A system is represented at level n if one only considers the joint distributions with k\leq n variables, ignoring higher-order joint distributions. We show that the level-wise contextuality analysis can be used in conjunction with any well-constructed measure of contextuality. We present a method of concatenated systems to explore contextuality profiles systematically, and we apply it to the contextuality profiles for three major measures of contextuality proposed in the literature.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new metric for analyzing contextuality profiles of systems using a curve rather than a single numerical degree.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide a more nuanced understanding of contextuality in systems, moving beyond a singular measure to a detailed curve-based approach.
AI.method: The authors introduce a level-wise analysis using a notion of contextuality profiles and apply it through a concatenated systems method with three existing measures.
AI.result: They demonstrate that their approach can systematically explore contextuality profiles, complementing existing measures.
AI.conclusion: Contextuality can be better understood using a level-wise curve approach, offering deeper insights compared to traditional single-measure methods.

title: Near-Optimal Primal-Dual Algorithm for Learning Linear Mixture CMDPs with Adversarial Rewards
summary: We study safe reinforcement learning in finite-horizon linear mixture constrained Markov decision processes (CMDPs) with adversarial rewards under full-information feedback and an unknown transition kernel. We propose a primal-dual policy optimization algorithm that achieves regret and constraint violation bounds of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{d^2 H^3 K})$ under mild conditions, where $d$ is the feature dimension, $H$ is the horizon, and $K$ is the number of episodes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first provably efficient algorithm for linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial rewards. In particular, our regret bound is near-optimal, matching the known minimax lower bound up to logarithmic factors. The key idea is to introduce a regularized dual update that enables a drift-based analysis. This step is essential, as strong duality-based analysis cannot be directly applied when reward functions change across episodes. In addition, we extend weighted ridge regression-based parameter estimation to the constrained setting, allowing us to construct tighter confidence intervals that are crucial for deriving the near-optimal regret bound.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new algorithm for safe reinforcement learning in constrained Markov decision processes under adversarial rewards, achieving competitive regret and constraint violation bounds.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of reinforcement learning in constrained environments where rewards are adversarial and transition kernels are unknown, while aiming to maintain safety efficiently.
AI.method: A primal-dual policy optimization algorithm with a regularized dual update and weighted ridge regression for parameter estimation, enabling tighter confidence intervals and drift-based analysis.
AI.result: Proven regret and constraint violation bounds of $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{d^2 H^3 K})$, achieving near-optimal performance in linear mixture CMDPs with adversarial rewards.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm is the first to achieve provable efficiency in the setting, matching known minimax lower bounds up to logarithmic factors, thus advancing safe reinforcement learning in complex environments.

title: Reasoning-Driven Anomaly Detection and Localization with Image-Level Supervision
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have recently demonstrated remarkable reasoning and perceptual abilities for anomaly detection. However, most approaches remain confined to image-level anomaly detection and textual reasoning, while pixel-level localization still relies on external vision modules and dense annotations. In this work, we activate the intrinsic reasoning potential of MLLMs to perform anomaly detection, pixel-level localization, and interpretable reasoning solely from image-level supervision, without any auxiliary components or pixel-wise labels. Specifically, we propose Reasoning-Driven Anomaly Localization (ReAL), which extracts anomaly-related tokens from the autoregressive reasoning process and aggregates their attention responses to produce pixel-level anomaly maps. We further introduce a Consistency-Guided Reasoning Optimization (CGRO) module that leverages reinforcement learning to align reasoning tokens with visual attentions, resulting in more coherent reasoning and accurate anomaly localization. Extensive experiments on four public benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly improves anomaly detection, localization, and interpretability. Remarkably, despite relying solely on image-level supervision, our approach achieves performance competitive with MLLM-based methods trained under dense pixel-level supervision. Code is available at https://github.com/YizhouJin313/ReADL.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "ReAL," which uses multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for anomaly detection and pixel-level localization without pixel-wise labels or external systems, leveraging interpretative reasoning.
AI.motivation: Current methods for anomaly detection focus on image-level detection and textual reasoning but rely on external systems or dense pixel annotations for detailed localization. This work seeks to eliminate this reliance and enhance intrinsic MLLM capabilities.
AI.method: The paper proposes "ReAL," which uses anomaly-related tokens from reasoning processes to generate pixel-level maps. A Consistency-Guided Reasoning Optimization (CGRO) module aligns reasoning tokens with visual attention through reinforcement learning.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms existing image-supervised approaches in anomaly detection and localization across four benchmarks, achieving competitive performance with pixel-supervised models.
AI.conclusion: The research validates the potential of MLLMs for both interpretable reasoning and effective pixel-level anomaly localization using only image-level supervision, establishing a new standard in anomaly detection.

title: Complementarity-Preserving Generative Theory for Multimodal ECG Synthesis: A Quantum-Inspired Approach
summary: Multimodal deep learning has substantially improved electrocardiogram (ECG) classification by jointly leveraging time, frequency, and time-frequency representations. However, existing generative models typically synthesize these modalities independently, resulting in synthetic ECG data that are visually plausible yet physiologically inconsistent across domains. This work establishes a Complementarity-Preserving Generative Theory (CPGT), which posits that physiologically valid multimodal signal generation requires explicit preservation of cross-domain complementarity rather than loosely coupled modality synthesis. We instantiate CPGT through Q-CFD-GAN, a quantum-inspired generative framework that models multimodal ECG structure within a complex-valued latent space and enforces complementarity-aware constraints regulating mutual information, redundancy, and morphological coherence. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that Q-CFD-GAN reduces latent embedding variance by 82%, decreases classifier-based plausibility error by 26.6%, and restores tri-domain complementarity from 0.56 to 0.91, while achieving the lowest observed morphology deviation (3.8%). These findings show that preserving multimodal information geometry, rather than optimizing modality-specific fidelity alone, is essential for generating synthetic ECG signals that remain physiologically meaningful and suitable for downstream clinical machine-learning applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a quantum-inspired generative model, Q-CFD-GAN, that generates physiologically consistent synthetic ECG data by preserving cross-domain complementarity across various modalities.
AI.motivation: Current generative models for ECG data fail to ensure physiological consistency across time, frequency, and time-frequency domains by treating them independently.
AI.method: The authors propose a Complementarity-Preserving Generative Theory (CPGT) and implement it using Q-CFD-GAN, which employs a quantum-inspired complex-valued latent space and adds constraints to enforce multimodal consistency.
AI.result: Q-CFD-GAN demonstrated reduced latent embedding variance by 82%, a 26.6% decrease in classifier-based plausibility error, and restoration of tri-domain complementarity from 0.56 to 0.91. It also achieved the lowest morphology deviation (3.8%).
AI.conclusion: Maintaining multimodal information geometry is crucial for producing physiologically meaningful synthetic ECG signals suitable for clinical machine-learning tasks.

title: Spectral Signatures of Data Quality: Eigenvalue Tail Index as a Diagnostic for Label Noise in Neural Networks
summary: We investigate whether spectral properties of neural network weight matrices can predict test accuracy. Under controlled label noise variation, the tail index alpha of the eigenvalue distribution at the network's bottleneck layer predicts test accuracy with leave-one-out R^2 = 0.984 (21 noise levels, 3 seeds per level), far exceeding all baselines: the best conventional metric (Frobenius norm of the optimal layer) achieves LOO R^2 = 0.149. This relationship holds across three architectures (MLP, CNN, ResNet-18) and two datasets (MNIST, CIFAR-10). However, under hyperparameter variation at fixed data quality (180 configurations varying width, depth, learning rate, and weight decay), all spectral and conventional measures are weak predictors (R^2 < 0.25), with simple baselines (global L_2 norm, LOO R^2 = 0.219) slightly outperforming spectral measures (tail alpha, LOO R^2 = 0.167). We therefore frame the tail index as a data quality diagnostic: a powerful detector of label corruption and training set degradation, rather than a universal generalization predictor. A noise detector calibrated on synthetic noise successfully identifies real human annotation errors in CIFAR-10N (9% noise detected with 3% error). We identify the information-processing bottleneck layer as the locus of this signature and connect the observations to the BBP phase transition in spiked random matrix models. We also report a negative result: the level spacing ratio <r> is uninformative for weight matrices due to Wishart universality.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how spectral properties of weight matrices in neural networks, specifically the tail index of eigenvalue distributions, correlate with test accuracy, showing strong predictive power under label noise but weaker under hyperparameter variation.
AI.motivation: To explore whether spectral properties of neural network weight matrices can serve as reliable predictors of test accuracy, with a particular interest in data quality signals like label noise.
AI.method: The study measured spectral properties like the tail index of weight matrices across various experimental setups, including label noise and hyperparameter variation, and evaluated their predictive power via leave-one-out R^2.
AI.result: The tail index accurately predicts test accuracy under label noise variations but performs poorly under hyperparameter variations. It excels as a diagnostic tool for label corruption, identifying annotation errors in real datasets like CIFAR-10N.
AI.conclusion: Spectral metrics, particularly the tail index, are valuable for diagnosing data quality issues like label noise but are not reliable as universal predictors of generalization performance.

title: Communicating about Space: Language-Mediated Spatial Integration Across Partial Views
summary: Humans build shared spatial understanding by communicating partial, viewpoint-dependent observations. We ask whether Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can do the same, aligning distinct egocentric views through dialogue to form a coherent, allocentric mental model of a shared environment. To study this systematically, we introduce COSMIC, a benchmark for Collaborative Spatial Communication. In this setting, two static MLLM agents observe a 3D indoor environment from different viewpoints and exchange natural-language messages to solve spatial queries. COSMIC contains 899 diverse scenes and 1250 question-answer pairs spanning five tasks. We find a consistent capability hierarchy, MLLMs are most reliable at identifying shared anchor objects across views, perform worse on relational reasoning, and largely fail at building globally consistent maps, performing near chance, even for the frontier models. Moreover, we find thinking capability yields consistent gains in anchor grounding, but is insufficient for higher-level spatial communication. To contextualize model behavior, we additionally collect 250 human-human dialogues. Humans achieve 95% aggregate accuracy, leaving significant room for improvement for even the best performing model Gemini-3-Pro-Thinking which achieves 72% aggregate accuracy. Moreover, human conversations become increasingly specific as partners converge on a shared mental model, whereas model dialogues continue to explore new possibilities rather than converging, consistent with a limited ability to build and maintain a robust shared mental model. Our code and data is available at https://github.com/ankursikarwar/Cosmic

AI.tldr: The paper examines if Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) can align different egocentric views into a shared spatial understanding through dialogue, using the COSMIC benchmark. Findings reveal models struggle with globally consistent spatial reasoning compared to humans.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether MLLMs can mimic human-like collaborative spatial understanding by aligning egocentric viewpoints through natural language communication.
AI.method: Introduced the COSMIC benchmark with 899 3D indoor scenes, 1250 question-answer pairs across five tasks. Two stationary MLLMs exchange messages to solve spatial queries, compared against human performance.
AI.result: MLLMs show varied reliability: strong at anchor object identification, weak at relational reasoning, and minimal competency in constructing consistent spatial maps compared to humans.
AI.conclusion: MLLMs show limited spatial reasoning abilities, failing to build robust shared mental models like humans. Human dialogues converge better, indicating significant gaps in model performance despite advancements.

title: Physicochemical-Neural Fusion for Semi-Closed-Circuit Respiratory Autonomy in Extreme Environments
summary: This paper introduces Galactic Bioware's Life Support System, a semi-closed-circuit breathing apparatus designed for integration into a positive-pressure firefighting suit and governed by an AI control system. The breathing loop incorporates a soda lime CO2 scrubber, a silica gel dehumidifier, and pure O2 replenishment with finite consumables. One-way exhaust valves maintain positive pressure while creating a semi-closed system in which outward venting gradually depletes the gas inventory. Part I develops the physicochemical foundations from first principles, including state-consistent thermochemistry, stoichiometric capacity limits, adsorption isotherms, and oxygen-management constraints arising from both fire safety and toxicity. Part II introduces an AI control architecture that fuses three sensor tiers, external environmental sensing, internal suit atmosphere sensing (with triple-redundant O2 cells and median voting), and firefighter biometrics. The controller combines receding-horizon model-predictive control (MPC) with a learned metabolic model and a reinforcement learning (RL) policy advisor, with all candidate actuator commands passing through a final control-barrier-function safety filter before reaching the hardware. This architecture is intended to optimize performance under unknown mission duration and exertion profiles. In this paper we introduce an 18-state, 3-control nonlinear state-space formulation using only sensors viable in structural firefighting, with triple-redundant O2 sensing and median voting. Finally, we introduce an MPC framework with a dynamic resource scarcity multiplier, an RL policy advisor for warm-starting, and a final control-barrier-function safety filter through which all actuator commands must pass, demonstrating 18-34% endurance improvement in simulation over PID baselines while maintaining tighter physiological and fire-safety margins.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a semi-closed-circuit life support system for firefighting suits, governed by a sophisticated AI framework for optimal performance.
AI.motivation: To enhance firefighter safety and suit endurance during unpredictable and high-stress scenarios by addressing oxygen management limitations of existing systems.
AI.method: The system combines a semi-closed-circuit breathing loop, advanced AI control (MPC with RL advisory), and safety filters, supported by a physicochemical framework and sensor integration.
AI.result: Simulation shows 18-34% endurance improvement over traditional systems with better safety and physiological margin maintenance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system effectively augments endurance while ensuring safety, marking significant progress in firefighter protective technology.

title: ATLAS-RTC: Closing the Loop on LLM Agent Output with Token-Level Runtime Control
summary: We present ATLAS-RTC, a runtime control system for autoregressive language models that enforces structured output during decoding. ATLAS-RTC monitors generation at each step, detects drift from output contracts using lightweight signals, and applies targeted interventions such as biasing, masking, and rollback. Unlike post-hoc validation or static constrained decoding, it operates in a closed loop, enabling correction before errors materialize. Across structured generation and tool-calling tasks, ATLAS-RTC improves first-attempt success rates by 20 to 37.8 percentage points, with up to 88% latency reduction in failure-dominated settings. Results show that many failures arise from decoding artifacts rather than task misunderstanding, motivating runtime control as a distinct layer in LLM systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ATLAS-RTC, a control system for language models that ensures structured outputs by monitoring and intervening during decoding.
AI.motivation: Address failures in language model generation arising from decoding artifacts rather than a lack of task understanding.
AI.method: ATLAS-RTC monitors language model generation for deviations from output contracts and performs in-loop interventions such as biasing, masking, or rollback.
AI.result: The approach improves first-attempt success rates in structured generation tasks by 20–37.8% and reduces latency in failure-heavy scenarios by up to 88%.
AI.conclusion: Runtime control offers significant benefits in mitigating decoding issues and represents a key addition to language model systems.

title: Incentivizing Temporal-Awareness in Egocentric Video Understanding Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have recently shown strong performance in visual understanding, yet they often lack temporal awareness, particularly in egocentric settings where reasoning depends on the correct ordering and evolution of events. This deficiency stems in part from training objectives that fail to explicitly reward temporal reasoning and instead rely on frame-level spatial shortcuts. To address this limitation, we propose Temporal Global Policy Optimization (TGPO), a reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) algorithm designed to incentivize temporal awareness in MLLMs. TGPO contrasts model outputs generated from temporally ordered versus shuffled video frames to derive calibrated, globally normalized reward signals that explicitly favor temporally coherent reasoning. Integrated with GRPO and GSPO, TGPO supports cold-start RL training and effectively suppresses spatial shortcut behaviors learned by existing MLLMs. Experiments across five egocentric video benchmarks demonstrate that TGPO consistently improves temporal grounding and causal coherence, outperforming prior RL-based video reasoning approaches. Our results suggest that TGPO offers a simple and scalable pathway toward temporally robust MLLMs for egocentric video understanding.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Temporal Global Policy Optimization (TGPO), an RL-based approach to enhance multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for temporal reasoning in egocentric video understanding, resolving their deficiency in handling temporal awareness.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs excel at visual understanding but struggle with temporal reasoning, especially in cases where event ordering is crucial, due to reliance on frame-level spatial shortcuts and inadequate training objectives.
AI.method: The authors propose TGPO, an RLVR-based algorithm using calibrated rewards to encourage temporal coherence. It contrasts outputs on ordered vs. shuffled frames and incorporates GRPO and GSPO for effective RL training.
AI.result: TGPO shows consistent improvement in temporal reasoning, outperforming earlier RL models in five egocentric video benchmarks by enhancing temporal grounding and causal coherence.
AI.conclusion: TGPO provides a scalable and effective framework to enhance temporal reasoning in MLLMs, advancing their capabilities in egocentric video understanding.

title: Path-Following Guidance for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle with Bounded Lateral Acceleration
summary: This paper addresses the three-dimensional path-following guidance problem for unmanned aerial vehicles under explicit actuator constraints. Unlike conventional approaches that assume unbounded control inputs or handle saturation heuristically, the proposed method incorporates bounded lateral acceleration directly into the guidance design. A nonlinear guidance framework is developed employing a nested saturation-based control technique. The proposed guidance strategy guarantees bounded control inputs while ensuring exponential convergence of cross-track errors to zero. The formulation is applicable to general smooth paths and is systematically extended from planar to three-dimensional scenarios using a path-tangent coordinate framework. Rigorous stability analysis based on Lyapunov theory establishes convergence and feasibility properties of the closed-loop system. Numerical simulations on representative paths, including straight-line, circular, and sinusoidal paths, demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior tracking performance, reduced control effort, and robustness against disturbances compared to existing guidance laws. The simplicity of the design and its compatibility with practical actuator limits make it suitable for real-world UAV applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a nonlinear guidance framework for UAVs that ensures bounded control inputs and achieves precise path-following performance in three-dimensional space.
AI.motivation: To address the limitations of conventional UAV guidance methods that assume unbounded control inputs or handle actuator saturation heuristically.
AI.method: A nested saturation-based control technique is employed within a nonlinear guidance framework, ensuring bounded lateral acceleration and exponential convergence of cross-track errors.
AI.result: The method achieves superior path tracking performance, reduced control efforts, and robustness against disturbances, as demonstrated through numerical simulations on multiple representative paths.
AI.conclusion: The proposed guidance strategy is simple, compatible with actuator limits, and suitable for real-world UAV applications due to its stability, feasibility, and effectiveness.

title: Deep Learning Multi-Horizon Irradiance Nowcasting: A Comparative Evaluation of Three Methods for Leveraging Sky Images
summary: We investigate three distinct methods of incorporating all-sky imager (ASI) images into deep learning (DL) irradiance nowcasting. The first method relies on a convolutional neural network (CNN) to extract features directly from raw RGB images. The second method uses state-of-the-art algorithms to engineer 2D feature maps informed by domain knowledge, e.g., cloud segmentation, the cloud motion vector, solar position, and cloud base height. These feature maps are then passed to a CNN to extract compound features. The final method relies on aggregating the engineered 2D feature maps into time-series input. Each of the three methods were then used as part of a DL model trained on a high-frequency, 29-day dataset to generate multi-horizon forecasts of global horizontal irradiance up to 15 minutes ahead. The models were then evaluated using root mean squared error and skill score on 7 selected days of data. Aggregated engineered ASI features as model input yielded superior forecasting performance, demonstrating that integration of ASI images into DL nowcasting models is possible without complex spatially-ordered DL-architectures and inputs, underscoring opportunities for alternative image processing methods as well as the potential for improved spatial DL feature processing methods.

AI.tldr: The study compares three methods to integrate all-sky imager (ASI) images into deep learning systems for irradiance nowcasting, concluding that engineered time-series features offer superior forecasting performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve solar irradiance forecasting using all-sky imager (ASI) images by exploring different ways of integrating ASI data into deep learning models.
AI.method: Three approaches were tested: directly extracting features from raw RGB ASI images using CNNs, creating engineered 2D feature maps using domain knowledge and then applying CNNs, and aggregating the engineered 2D features into time-series inputs.
AI.result: The time-series aggregation of engineered ASI features performed the best in forecasting irradiance, as measured by root mean squared error and skill score over multi-horizon forecasts.
AI.conclusion: ASI image data can improve irradiance nowcasting in deep learning models, with engineered time-series features providing the most effective results while simplifying the spatial processing requirements.

title: MotionRFT: Unified Reinforcement Fine-Tuning for Text-to-Motion Generation
summary: Text-to-motion generation has advanced with diffusion- and flow-based generative models, yet supervised pretraining remains insufficient to align models with high-level objectives such as semantic consistency, realism, and human preference. Existing post-training methods have key limitations: they (1) target a specific motion representation, such as joints, (2) optimize a particular aspect, such as text-motion alignment, and may compromise other factors; and (3) incur substantial computational overhead, data dependence, and coarse-grained optimization. We present a reinforcement fine-tuning framework that comprises a heterogeneous-representation, multi-dimensional reward model, MotionReward, and an efficient, fine-grained fine-tuning method, EasyTune. To obtain a unified semantics representation, MotionReward maps heterogeneous motions into a shared semantic space anchored by text, enabling multidimensional reward learning; Self-refinement Preference Learning further enhances semantics without additional annotations. For efficient and effective fine-tuning, we identify the recursive gradient dependence across denoising steps as the key bottleneck, and propose EasyTune, which optimizes step-wise rather than over the full trajectory, yielding dense, fine-grained, and memory-efficient updates. Extensive experiments validate the effectiveness of our framework, achieving FID 0.132 at 22.10 GB peak memory for MLD model and saving up to 15.22 GB over DRaFT. It reduces FID by 22.9% on joint-based ACMDM, and achieves a 12.6% R-Precision gain and 23.3% FID improvement on rotation-based HY Motion. Our project page with code is publicly available.

AI.tldr: A framework combining a multidimensional reward model (MotionReward) and an efficient fine-tuning method (EasyTune) was proposed to enhance text-to-motion generation. It balances semantic consistency, realism, and human preference while reducing computational overhead and memory usage.
AI.motivation: To address the insufficiencies in supervised pretraining for text-to-motion generation, ensuring models align with high-level objectives (semantic consistency, realism, human preference) and overcoming the limitations of existing post-training methods.
AI.method: The proposed framework includes MotionReward, which aligns diverse motion representations in a shared semantic space, and EasyTune, a step-wise fine-tuning method that avoids computational bottlenecks for efficient optimization.
AI.result: The framework reduces memory usage and improves FID and R-Precision metrics significantly across various datasets, showing better semantic alignment and realism than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: The introduced reinforcement fine-tuning setup demonstrates its ability to overcome existing limitations in text-to-motion generation effectively, offering memory-efficient and high-quality results.

title: CRISP: Characterizing Relative Impact of Scholarly Publications
summary: Assessing a cited paper's impact is typically done by analyzing its citation context in isolation within the citing paper. While this focuses on the most directly relevant text, it prevents relative comparisons across all the works a paper cites. We propose CRISP, which instead jointly ranks all cited papers within a citing paper using large language models (LLMs). To mitigate LLMs' positional bias, we rank each list three times in a randomized order and aggregate the impact labels through majority voting. This joint approach leverages the full citation context, rather than evaluating citations independently, to more reliably distinguish impactful references. CRISP outperforms a prior state-of-the-art impact classifier by +9.5% accuracy and +8.3% F1 on a dataset of human-annotated citations. CRISP further gains efficiency through fewer LLM calls and performs competitively with an open-source model, enabling scalable, cost-effective citation impact analysis. We release our rankings, impact labels, and codebase to support future research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CRISP, a method leveraging LLMs to jointly rank paper citations based on impact, outperforming previous methods with improved accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing citation impact analyses often evaluate citations individually, missing overall contextual comparisons. The authors aim to provide a more reliable and scalable alternative.
AI.method: CRISP uses large language models (LLMs) to jointly rank all cited papers in a citing document, mitigating positional biases through randomized ranking and majority voting aggregation.
AI.result: CRISP demonstrates a +9.5% increase in accuracy and +8.3% in F1 scores on human-annotated data compared to the prior state-of-the-art method. It also achieves efficiency with fewer LLM calls.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CRISP method advances citation impact analysis with improved reliability and cost-effectiveness, while releasing resources to aid future research.

title: Structured Observation Language for Efficient and Generalizable Vision-Language Navigation
summary: Vision-Language Navigation (VLN) requires an embodied agent to navigate complex environments by following natural language instructions, which typically demands tight fusion of visual and language modalities. Existing VLN methods often convert raw images into visual tokens or implicit features, requiring large-scale visual pre-training and suffering from poor generalization under environmental variations (e.g., lighting, texture). To address these issues, we propose SOL-Nav (Structured Observation Language for Navigation), a novel framework that translates egocentric visual observations into compact structured language descriptions for efficient and generalizable navigation. Specifically, we divide RGB-D images into a N*N grid, extract representative semantic, color, and depth information for each grid cell to form structured text, and concatenate this with the language instruction as pure language input to a pre-trained language model (PLM). Experimental results on standard VLN benchmarks (R2R, RxR) and real-world deployments demonstrate that SOL-Nav significantly reduces the model size and training data dependency, fully leverages the reasoning and representation capabilities of PLMs, and achieves strong generalization to unseen environments.

AI.tldr: SOL-Nav introduces a method that converts visual observations into structured textual descriptions, enabling more efficient and generalizable vision-language navigation (VLN).
AI.motivation: Improve VLN generalization and efficiency by addressing issues with visual tokens and implicit features, which struggle with environmental variations.
AI.method: Converts RGB-D images into structured text (semantic, color, depth info for grid cells) and integrates this with language instructions as input to pre-trained language models.
AI.result: Experimental results show reduced model size, decreased training data dependency, and better generalization to unseen environments in VLN benchmarks and real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SOL-Nav effectively utilizes structured language representations to enhance VLN tasks, leveraging pre-trained language models for improved adaptation and performance.

title: PI-Mamba: Linear-Time Protein Backbone Generation via Spectrally Initialized Flow Matching
summary:
Motivation: Generative models for protein backbone design have to simultaneously ensure geometric validity, sampling efficiency, and scalability to long sequences. However, most existing approaches rely on iterative refinement, quadratic attention mechanisms, or post-hoc geometry correction, leading to a persistent trade-off between computational efficiency and structural fidelity.
  Results: We present Physics-Informed Mamba (PI-Mamba), a generative model that enforces exact local covalent geometry by construction while enabling linear-time inference. PI-Mamba integrates a differentiable constraint-enforcement operator into a flow-matching framework and couples it with a Mamba-based state-space architecture. To improve optimisation stability and backbone realism, we introduce a spectral initialization derived from the Rouse polymer model and an auxiliary cis-proline awareness head. Across benchmark tasks, PI-Mamba achieves 0.0\% local geometry violations and high designability (scTM = $0.91\pm 0.03$, n = 100), while scaling to proteins exceeding 2,000 residues on a single A5000 GPU (24 GB).

AI.tldr: This paper introduces PI-Mamba, a generative model for protein backbone design that ensures geometric validity, sampling efficiency, and scalability, outperforming existing techniques.
AI.motivation: Existing models for protein backbone design struggle with a trade-off between computational efficiency and structural fidelity, arising from iterative refinement, quadratic attention, or post-hoc geometry corrections.
AI.method: PI-Mamba is a generative model using a differentiable constraint-enforcement operator within a flow-matching framework, integrates Mamba-based architecture, employs spectral initialization from the Rouse polymer model, and features a cis-proline awareness head.
AI.result: PI-Mamba eliminates local geometry violations, achieves high designability scores (scTM = $0.91\pm 0.03$), and can scale to proteins over 2,000 residues on a single A5000 GPU.
AI.conclusion: PI-Mamba successfully balances computational efficiency and structural fidelity in protein backbone design, demonstrating scalability and high accuracy.

title: Physics-Guided Transformer (PGT): Physics-Aware Attention Mechanism for PINNs
summary: Reconstructing continuous physical fields from sparse, irregular observations is a central challenge in scientific machine learning, particularly for systems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs). Existing physics-informed methods typically enforce governing equations as soft penalty terms during optimization, often leading to gradient imbalance, instability, and degraded physical consistency under limited data. We introduce the Physics-Guided Transformer (PGT), a neural architecture that embeds physical structure directly into the self-attention mechanism. Specifically, PGT incorporates a heat-kernel-derived additive bias into attention logits, encoding diffusion dynamics and temporal causality within the representation. Query coordinates attend to these physics-conditioned context tokens, and the resulting features are decoded using a FiLM-modulated sinusoidal implicit network that adaptively controls spectral response. We evaluate PGT on the one-dimensional heat equation and two-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes systems. In sparse 1D reconstruction with 100 observations, PGT achieves a relative L2 error of 5.9e-3, significantly outperforming both PINNs and sinusoidal representations. In the 2D cylinder wake problem, PGT uniquely achieves both low PDE residual (8.3e-4) and competitive relative error (0.034), outperforming methods that optimize only one objective. These results demonstrate that embedding physics within attention improves stability, generalization, and physical fidelity under data-scarce conditions.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Physics-Guided Transformer (PGT), a neural architecture that integrates physical principles into its attention mechanism to better reconstruct physical fields from sparse data governed by PDEs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address shortcomings in current physics-informed machine learning methods, such as gradient imbalance, instability, and poor physical consistency under sparse data conditions.
AI.method: The method involves embedding physical structure into the PGT's self-attention mechanism via a heat-kernel-derived bias, and utilizing a FiLM-modulated sinusoidal implicit network for decoding to adaptively control spectral response.
AI.result: PGT achieves superior performance on the 1D heat equation and 2D Navier-Stokes systems, with lower errors compared to traditional methods like PINNs, demonstrating improved stability and accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Embedding physics within the self-attention mechanism enhances the stability, generalization, and physical fidelity of machine learning models under sparse observation scenarios.

title: K$α$LOS finds Consensus: A Meta-Algorithm for Evaluating Inter-Annotator Agreement in Complex Vision Tasks
comment: Accepted at CVPR 2026. Also known as KALOS
summary:
Progress in object detection benchmarks is stagnating. It is limited not by architectures but by the inability to distinguish model improvements from label noise. To restore trust in benchmarking the field requires rigorous quantification of annotation consistency to ensure the reliability of evaluation data. However, standard statistical metrics fail to handle the instance correspondence problem inherent to vision tasks. Furthermore, validating new agreement metrics remains circular because no objective ground truth for agreement exists. This forces reliance on unverifiable heuristics.
  We propose K$α$LOS (KALOS), a unified meta-algorithm that generalizes the "Localization First" principle to standardize dataset quality evaluation. By resolving spatial correspondence before assessing agreement, our framework transforms complex spatio-categorical problems into nominal reliability matrices. Unlike prior heuristic implementations, K$α$LOS employs a principled, data-driven configuration; by statistically calibrating the localization parameters to the inherent agreement distribution, it generalizes to diverse tasks ranging from bounding boxes to volumetric segmentation or pose estimation. This standardization enables granular diagnostics beyond a single score. These include annotator vitality, collaboration clustering, and localization sensitivity. To validate this approach, we introduce a novel and empirically derived noise generator. Where prior validations relied on uniform error assumptions, our controllable testbed models complex and non-isotropic human variability. This provides evidence of the metric's properties and establishes K$α$LOS as a robust standard for distinguishing signal from noise in modern computer vision benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses stagnation in object detection benchmarks due to label noise and proposes K$α$LOS, a standardized meta-algorithm, to reliably evaluate and diagnose dataset quality.
AI.motivation: The motivation comes from the stagnation in object detection progress, largely due to an inability to differentiate between model improvements and label noise.
AI.method: The authors propose K$α$LOS, a unified meta-algorithm that standardizes dataset quality evaluation by resolving spatial correspondence before judging agreement, and employs a data-driven configuration.
AI.result: K$α$LOS accurately diagnoses dataset quality issues and generalizes across tasks, from bounding boxes to volumetric segmentation, supported by validation using a novel noise generator modeling complex human behavior.
AI.conclusion: K$α$LOS provides a robust and standardized method to reliably evaluate annotation consistency in computer vision, addressing the limitations of prior heuristics and metrics.

title: GroupRAG: Cognitively Inspired Group-Aware Retrieval and Reasoning via Knowledge-Driven Problem Structuring
summary: The performance of language models is commonly limited by insufficient knowledge and constrained reasoning. Prior approaches such as Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) address these issues by incorporating external knowledge or enforcing linear reasoning chains, but often degrade in real-world settings. Inspired by cognitive science, which characterizes human problem solving as search over structured problem spaces rather than single inference chains, we argue that inadequate awareness of problem structure is a key overlooked limitation. We propose GroupRAG, a cognitively inspired, group-aware retrieval and reasoning framework based on knowledge-driven keypoint grouping. GroupRAG identifies latent structural groups within a problem and performs retrieval and reasoning from multiple conceptual starting points, enabling fine-grained interaction between the two processes. Experiments on MedQA show that GroupRAG outperforms representative RAG- and CoT-based baselines. These results suggest that explicitly modeling problem structure, as inspired by human cognition, is a promising direction for robust retrieval-augmented reasoning.

AI.tldr: GroupRAG is a retrieval and reasoning framework that incorporates structured problem understanding inspired by human cognition to outperform traditional methods in tasks like MedQA.
AI.motivation: The paper identifies limitations in existing language model methods like RAG and CoT, particularly their inability to adequately handle structured problem-solving as humans do.
AI.method: The authors introduce GroupRAG, which uses knowledge-driven keypoint grouping to identify structural groups in a problem and facilitates retrieval and reasoning from multiple conceptual points.
AI.result: GroupRAG demonstrates superior performance compared to standard RAG and CoT methods in the MedQA benchmark, showcasing its effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Explicit modeling of problem structure, inspired by human cognition, enhances retrieval-augmented reasoning and addresses prior limitations inherent in traditional methods.

title: E-TIDE: Fast, Structure-Preserving Motion Forecasting from Event Sequences
summary: Event-based cameras capture visual information as asynchronous streams of per-pixel brightness changes, generating sparse, temporally precise data. Compared to conventional frame-based sensors, they offer significant advantages in capturing high-speed dynamics while consuming substantially less power. Predicting future event representations from past observations is an important problem, enabling downstream tasks such as future semantic segmentation or object tracking without requiring access to future sensor measurements. While recent state-of-the-art approaches achieve strong performance, they often rely on computationally heavy backbones and, in some cases, large-scale pretraining, limiting their applicability in resource-constrained scenarios. In this work, we introduce E-TIDE, a lightweight, end-to-end trainable architecture for event-tensor prediction that is designed to operate efficiently without large-scale pretraining. Our approach employs the TIDE module (Temporal Interaction for Dynamic Events), motivated by efficient spatiotemporal interaction design for sparse event tensors, to capture temporal dependencies via large-kernel mixing and activity-aware gating while maintaining low computational complexity. Experiments on standard event-based datasets demonstrate that our method achieves competitive performance with significantly reduced model size and training requirements, making it well-suited for real-time deployment under tight latency and memory budgets.

AI.tldr: Event-based cameras stream sparse, asynchronous brightness changes; E-TIDE predicts future event-tensors efficiently without heavy computation or pretraining.
AI.motivation: Address the need for efficient and lightweight methods to predict future event representations from past data in event-based cameras for resource-limited scenarios.
AI.method: Proposed E-TIDE architecture uses TIDE module for spatiotemporal interaction with large-kernel mixing and activity-aware gating, ensuring low computational complexity.
AI.result: E-TIDE achieves competitive performance on event-based datasets with reduced model size and training requirements compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: E-TIDE is a resource-efficient, real-time-capable solution for event-tensor prediction, suitable for scenarios with tight latency and memory constraints.

title: Scaling Atomistic Protein Binder Design with Generative Pretraining and Test-Time Compute
summary: Protein interaction modeling is central to protein design, which has been transformed by machine learning with applications in drug discovery and beyond. In this landscape, structure-based de novo binder design is cast as either conditional generative modeling or sequence optimization via structure predictors ("hallucination"). We argue that this is a false dichotomy and propose Proteina-Complexa, a novel fully atomistic binder generation method unifying both paradigms. We extend recent flow-based latent protein generation architectures and leverage the domain-domain interactions of monomeric computationally predicted protein structures to construct Teddymer, a new large-scale dataset of synthetic binder-target pairs for pretraining. Combined with high-quality experimental multimers, this enables training a strong base model. We then perform inference-time optimization with this generative prior, unifying the strengths of previously distinct generative and hallucination methods. Proteina-Complexa sets a new state of the art in computational binder design benchmarks: it delivers markedly higher in-silico success rates than existing generative approaches, and our novel test-time optimization strategies greatly outperform previous hallucination methods under normalized compute budgets. We also demonstrate interface hydrogen bond optimization, fold class-guided binder generation, and extensions to small molecule targets and enzyme design tasks, again surpassing prior methods. Code, models and new data will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: Proteina-Complexa is a novel protein binder generation method that unifies generative modeling and optimization, achieving state-of-the-art results in computational binder design.
AI.motivation: Current protein design approaches are split between generative modeling and sequence optimization, which the authors argue is a false dichotomy.
AI.method: Proteina-Complexa employs a flow-based protein generation architecture trained on a new dataset (Teddymer) and experimental data, followed by inference-time optimization to unify generative and optimization approaches.
AI.result: The approach achieves state-of-the-art benchmarks in protein binder design, surpassing previous generative and hallucination methods, while also showing versatility in diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method unifies the strengths of previously distinct approaches, demonstrating superior performance and broader applicability in protein design, with all resources to be made publicly available.

title: Let Triggers Control: Frequency-Aware Dropout for Effective Token Control
comment: CVPR 2026 P13N: Personalization in Generative AI workshop
summary: Text-to-image models such as Stable Diffusion have achieved unprecedented levels of high-fidelity visual synthesis. As these models advance, personalization of generative models -- commonly facilitated through Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with a dedicated trigger token -- has become a significant area of research. Previous works have naively assumed that fine-tuning with a single trigger token to represent new concepts. However, this often results in poor controllability, where the trigger token alone fails to reliably evoke the intended concept. We attribute this issue to the frequent co-occurrence of the trigger token with the surrounding context during fine-tuning, which entangles their representations and compromises the token's semantic distinctiveness. To disentangle this, we propose Frequency-Aware Dropout (FAD) -- a novel regularization technique that improves prompt controllability without adding new parameters. FAD consists of two key components: co-occurrence analysis and curriculum-inspired scheduling. Qualitative and quantitative analyses across token-based diffusion models (SD~1.5 and SDXL) and natural language--driven backbones (FLUX and Qwen-Image) demonstrate consistent gains in prompt fidelity, stylistic precision, and user-perceived quality. Our method provides a simple yet effective dropout strategy that enhances controllability and personalization in text-to-image generation. Notably, it achieves these improvements without introducing additional parameters or architectural modifications, making it readily applicable to existing models with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Frequency-Aware Dropout (FAD), a regularization technique for fine-tuning text-to-image generation models to improve prompt controllability and personalization.
AI.motivation: The motivation comes from the lack of controllability in text-to-image models when fine-tuning with a single trigger token. Trigger tokens often become entangled with surrounding context, reducing their semantic distinctiveness.
AI.method: The paper proposes Frequency-Aware Dropout (FAD), which includes co-occurrence analysis and curriculum-inspired scheduling. This technique aims to improve token distinctiveness by avoiding context entanglement during fine-tuning.
AI.result: Through qualitative and quantitative analysis across multiple model types, FAD demonstrated improved performance in prompt fidelity, stylistic precision, and user-perceived quality without adding new parameters.
AI.conclusion: FAD offers a simple and computationally efficient solution to enhance controllability and personalization in text-to-image models, making it well-suited for existing systems.

title: In your own words: computationally identifying interpretable themes in free-text survey data
summary: Free-text survey responses can provide nuance often missed by structured questions, but remain difficult to statistically analyze. To address this, we introduce In Your Own Words, a computational framework for exploratory analyses of free-text survey data that identifies structured, interpretable themes in free-text responses more precisely than previous computational approaches, facilitating systematic analysis. To illustrate the benefits of this approach, we apply it to a new dataset of free-text descriptions of race, gender, and sexual orientation from 1,004 U.S. participants. The themes our approach learns have three practical applications in survey research. First, the themes can suggest structured questions to add to future surveys by surfacing salient constructs -- such as belonging and identity fluidity -- that existing surveys do not capture. Second, the themes reveal heterogeneity within standardized categories, explaining additional variation in health, well-being, and identity importance. Third, the themes illuminate systematic discordance between self-identified and perceived identities, highlighting mechanisms of misrecognition that existing measures do not reflect. More broadly, our framework can be deployed in a wide range of survey settings to identify interpretable themes from free text, complementing existing qualitative methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a computational framework called 'In Your Own Words' to find themes in free-text survey data for better analysis.
AI.motivation: Analyzing free-text survey responses is challenging but can offer unique insights that structured questions often miss.
AI.method: A computational framework that identifies themes in free-text survey data more precisely than prior approaches.
AI.result: The approach identifies themes that suggest new structured survey questions, reveal heterogeneity in data, and uncover misrecognition mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a systematic way to analyze free-text survey responses, complementing qualitative methods and enabling broader applications.

title: Engineering Mythology: A Digital-Physical Framework for Culturally-Inspired Public Art
summary: Navagunjara Reborn: The Phoenix of Odisha was built for Burning Man 2025 as both a sculpture and an experiment-a fusion of myth, craft, and computation. This paper describes the digital-physical workflow developed for the project: a pipeline that linked digital sculpting, distributed fabrication by artisans in Odisha (India), modular structural optimization in the U.S., iterative feedback through photogrammetry and digital twins, and finally, one-shot full assembly at the art site in Black Rock Desert, Nevada. The desert installation tested not just materials, but also systems of collaboration: between artisans and engineers, between myth and technology, between cultural specificity and global experimentation. We share the lessons learned in design, fabrication, and deployment and offer a framework for future interdisciplinary projects at the intersection of cultural heritage, STEAM education, and public art. In retrospect, this workflow can be read as a convergence of many knowledge systems-artisan practice, structural engineering, mythic narrative, and environmental constraint-rather than as execution of a single fixed blueprint.

AI.tldr: The paper describes the creation of the sculpture "Navagunjara Reborn" for Burning Man 2025, combining myth, art, and technology with a unique digital-physical workflow spanning global collaboration.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to explore and document a multidisciplinary workflow uniting traditional craft, structural engineering, and modern computation to create a culturally significant and globally collaborative art installation.
AI.method: A comprehensive workflow involved digital sculpting, distributed fabrication by Odisha artisans, modular optimization in the U.S., photogrammetry feedback, digital twins, and a final desert installation.
AI.result: The project successfully integrated art and engineering, tested collaborative systems between artisans and engineers, and highlighted interactions between cultural heritage and global experimentation.
