AI.motivation: The paper focuses on improving regret minimization under non-stationarity, where the best expert may switch over time—a scenario not well-addressed by static regret targeted methods.
AI.method: The proposed method, PCGS, is augmented with a causal Transformer (PCGS-TF) as an update controller. It adapts loss updates dynamically to minimize regret across time-varying scenarios.
AI.result: PCGS-TF demonstrated superior performance in synthetic and real-world benchmarks, achieving the lowest mean dynamic regret across various scenarios and expert pool sizes.
AI.conclusion: PCGS-TF effectively handles non-stationarity and dynamic environments, showing promise in reducing dynamic regret under varying learning conditions and expert paths.

title: EVA: Bridging Performance and Human Alignment in Hard-Attention Vision Models for Image Classification
summary: Optimizing vision models purely for classification accuracy can impose an alignment tax, degrading human-like scanpaths and limiting interpretability. We introduce EVA, a neuroscience-inspired hard-attention mechanistic testbed that makes the performance-human-likeness trade-off explicit and adjustable. EVA samples a small number of sequential glimpses using a minimal fovea-periphery representation with CNN-based feature extractor and integrates variance control and adaptive gating to stabilize and regulate attention dynamics. EVA is trained with the standard classification objective without gaze supervision. On CIFAR-10 with dense human gaze annotations, EVA improves scanpath alignment under established metrics such as DTW, NSS, while maintaining competitive accuracy. Ablations show that CNN-based feature extraction drives accuracy but suppresses human-likeness, whereas variance control and gating restore human-aligned trajectories with minimal performance loss. We further validate EVA's scalability on ImageNet-100 and evaluate scanpath alignment on COCO-Search18 without COCO-Search18 gaze supervision or finetuning, where EVA yields human-like scanpaths on natural scenes without additional training. Overall, EVA provides a principled framework for trustworthy, human-interpretable active vision.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EVA, a neuroscience-inspired attention model optimizing human-like scanpaths and competitive accuracy without gaze supervision.
AI.motivation: To address the trade-off between classification accuracy and human-like scanpath alignment, which hinders interpretability and human-comprehensibility in vision models.
AI.method: EVA employs a minimal fovea-periphery representation, CNN-based feature extraction, variance control, and adaptive gating to stabilize attention dynamics. It is trained using standard classification objectives without requiring gaze supervision.
AI.result: EVA achieves improved scanpath alignment on benchmarks like CIFAR-10 and COCO-Search18 while maintaining competitive classification accuracy. Ablations show the individual contributions of its components to accuracy and human-likeness.
AI.conclusion: EVA demonstrates a scalable, interpretable, and human-aligned active vision framework, suitable for tasks requiring trustworthy predictions and human-like behavior in scanpaths.

title: IsoQuant: Hardware-Aligned SO(4) Isoclinic Rotations for LLM KV Cache Compression
summary:
Orthogonal feature decorrelation is effective for low-bit online vector quantization, but dense random orthogonal transforms incur prohibitive $O(d^2)$ storage and compute. RotorQuant reduces this cost with blockwise $3$D Clifford rotors, yet the resulting $3$D partition is poorly aligned with modern hardware and offers limited local mixing.
  We propose \textbf{IsoQuant}, a blockwise rotation framework based on quaternion algebra and the isoclinic decomposition of $SO(4)$. It represents each $4$D block as a quaternion and applies a closed-form transform $T(v)=q_L v \overline{q_R}$. This yields two main variants: \emph{IsoQuant-Full}, which realizes the full $SO(4)$ rotation, and \emph{IsoQuant-Fast}, which keeps only one isoclinic factor for lower cost; the framework also admits a lightweight $2$D special case. At $d=128$, IsoQuant-Full reduces forward rotation cost from about $2{,}408$ FMAs in RotorQuant to $1{,}024$, while IsoQuant-Fast further reduces it to $512$. Across $18$ fused CUDA settings with $d \in {128,256,512}$, bit widths ${2,3,4}$, and FP16/FP32 execution, IsoQuant achieves mean kernel-level speedups of about $4.5\times$--$4.7\times$ over RotorQuant while maintaining comparable reconstruction MSE, with peak speedups above $6\times$. Current validation is limited to the stage-1 quantize--dequantize path on synthetic normalized vectors; end-to-end KV-cache evaluation remains future work.

AI.tldr: IsoQuant is a novel blockwise rotation framework for low-bit online vector quantization, leveraging quaternion algebra for improved performance and reduced computational cost compared to RotorQuant.
AI.motivation: Reducing the prohibitive $O(d^2)$ storage and compute cost of dense random orthogonal transforms in low-bit online vector quantization while maintaining effective feature decorrelation.
AI.method: The method involves using quaternion algebra and the isoclinic decomposition of $SO(4)$ to represent $4$D blocks as quaternions. Closed-form transformations are applied, offering two variants: IsoQuant-Full (full $SO(4)$ rotation) and IsoQuant-Fast (reduced cost via a single isoclinic factor). A $2$D lightweight special case is also explored.
AI.result: IsoQuant significantly reduces computational cost, achieving a mean kernel-level speedup of $4.5\times$–$4.7\times$ and peak speedups exceeding $6\times$ over RotorQuant in $18$ CUDA settings.
AI.conclusion: IsoQuant demonstrates superior computational efficiency while maintaining comparable reconstruction MSE to RotorQuant. However, end-to-end KV-cache evaluation is deferred to future studies.

title: TerraSeg: Self-Supervised Ground Segmentation for Any LiDAR
summary: LiDAR perception is fundamental to robotics, enabling machines to understand their environment in 3D. A crucial task for LiDAR-based scene understanding and navigation is ground segmentation. However, existing methods are either handcrafted for specific sensor configurations or rely on costly per-point manual labels, severely limiting their generalization and scalability. To overcome this, we introduce TerraSeg, the first self-supervised, domain-agnostic model for LiDAR ground segmentation. We train TerraSeg on OmniLiDAR, a unified large-scale dataset that aggregates and standardizes data from 12 major public benchmarks. Spanning almost 22 million raw scans across 15 distinct sensor models, OmniLiDAR provides unprecedented diversity for learning a highly generalizable ground model. To supervise training without human annotations, we propose PseudoLabeler, a novel module that generates high-quality ground and non-ground labels through self-supervised per-scan runtime optimization. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that, despite using no manual labels, TerraSeg achieves state-of-the-art results on nuScenes, SemanticKITTI, and Waymo Perception while delivering real-time performance. Our code and model weights are publicly available.

AI.tldr: TerraSeg introduces a self-supervised ground segmentation model for LiDAR perception, trained on a diverse dataset without manual labels, achieving state-of-the-art results with real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Existing LiDAR ground segmentation methods are limited by reliance on handcrafted designs or costly manual labels, restricting generalization and scalability.
AI.method: TerraSeg employs self-supervised learning powered by its PseudoLabeler module and trains on OmniLiDAR, a dataset aggregating data from 12 major benchmarks.
AI.result: TerraSeg achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on major benchmarks like nuScenes, SemanticKITTI, and Waymo Perception, all while maintaining real-time capabilities.
AI.conclusion: TerraSeg demonstrates that self-supervised domain-agnostic modeling can surpass traditional methods reliant on manual annotations, offering scalable, generalizable solutions for LiDAR-based robotics.

title: ERPO: Token-Level Entropy-Regulated Policy Optimization for Large Reasoning Models
summary: Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) has significantly advanced the reasoning capabilities of large language models. However, standard Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) typically assigns a uniform, sequence-level advantage to all tokens, thereby overlooking the intrinsic information heterogeneity along reasoning chains. We show that this coarse-grained credit assignment leads to premature entropy collapse and encourages the model to generate redundant, low-quality reasoning paths. Through systematic empirical analysis, we identify Critical Decision Pivots (CDPs): transient high-entropy states where the policy's trajectory is most sensitive to perturbations. These pivots represent the "forks in the road" where effective multi-path exploration is most crucial yet often suppressed by uniform advantage signals. Building on these insights, we propose Entropy-Regulated Policy Optimization (ERPO), which transitions the optimization focus from coarse sequences to fine-grained token dynamics. ERPO introduces three synergistic components: (i) Entropy-aware Gating, which adaptively amplifies exploration at CDPs to facilitate diverse path discovery; (ii) Bucket-based Implicit Normalization, which mitigates difficulty bias by aligning token progress windows; and (iii) Result-anchored Advantage Synthesis, which re-weights token-level signals via outcome-driven anchors. Extensive experiments on competitive mathematical benchmarks (e.g., MATH, AIME) demonstrate that ERPO significantly outperforms GRPO. Notably, ERPO not only boosts reasoning accuracy but also yields significantly more concise and robust derivation paths, establishing a new efficiency-accuracy frontier for large reasoning models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ERPO, a method for better credit assignment in reinforcement learning systems for language models, showing superior performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of standard GRPO, which uses uniform credit assignment and leads to low-quality reasoning in large language models.
AI.method: The method, ERPO, incorporates three elements: (i) Entropy-aware Gating to enhance exploration, (ii) Bucket-based Implicit Normalization to reduce difficulty bias, and (iii) Result-anchored Advantage Synthesis for outcome-driven re-weighting.
AI.result: Empirical analysis on benchmarks like MATH and AIME shows that ERPO improves accuracy and generates more concise, high-quality reasoning paths compared to GRPO.
AI.conclusion: ERPO offers a fine-grained optimization approach, leading to more efficient and accurate results in reasoning tasks for large language models, setting a new standard for such systems.

title: Moving Beyond Review: Applying Language Models to Planning and Translation in Reflection
summary: Reflective writing is known to support the development of students' metacognitive skills, yet learners often struggle to engage in deep reflection, limiting learning gains. Although large language models (LLMs) have been shown to improve writing skills, their use as conversational agents for reflective writing has produced mixed results and has largely focused on providing feedback on reflective texts, rather than support during planning and organizing. In this paper, inspired by the Cognitive Process Theory of writing (CPT), we propose the first application of LLMs to the planning and translation steps of reflective writing. We introduce Pensée, a tool to explore the effects of explicit AI support during these stages by scaffolding structured reflection planning using a conversational agent, and supporting translation by automatically extracting key concepts. We evaluate Pensée in a controlled between-subjects experiment (N=93), manipulating AI support across writing phases. Results show significantly greater reflection depth and structural quality when learners receive support during planning and translation stages of CPT, though these effects reduce in a delayed post-test. Analyses of learner behavior and perceptions further illustrate how CPT-aligned conversational support shapes reflection processes and learner experience, contributing empirical evidence for theory-driven uses of LLMs in AI-supported reflective writing.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces "Pensée," a tool leveraging large language models (LLMs) to improve reflective writing by aiding planning and translation phases based on the Cognitive Process Theory of writing (CPT).
AI.motivation: Students often struggle with deep reflective writing, limiting learning outcomes, and current uses of LLMs focus primarily on feedback rather than assisting in initial planning and structuring.
AI.method: Inspired by CPT, the authors designed Pensée, a conversational AI tool that scaffolds planning and organizes reflective writing by extracting key concepts. Its effectiveness was tested through a controlled experiment with 93 participants, varying AI support across writing phases.
AI.result: Results indicate significant improvements in reflection depth and structural quality when learners received AI support during CPT stages (planning and translation), though the benefits decreased in delayed post-tests.
AI.conclusion: The study provides empirical evidence for theory-driven applications of LLMs in reflective writing, highlighting how CPT-aligned AI support can enhance the process and learner experience, particularly in planning and organizing stages.

title: Variational Neurons in Transformers for Language Modeling
summary:
Transformers for language modeling usually rely on deterministic internal computation, with uncertainty expressed mainly at the output layer. We introduce variational neurons into Transformer feed-forward computation so that uncertainty becomes part of the internal computation itself. Concretely, we replace deterministic feed-forward units with local variational units based on EVE while preserving the overall Transformer backbone.
  We evaluate this design in compact next-token language-modeling settings. We compare deterministic and variational variants with both predictive and probabilistic criteria. Alongside negative log-likelihood, perplexity and accuracy, we analyze calibration, conditional variance, mutual information and latent-usage statistics. The resulting picture is clear. Variational neurons integrate stably into Transformers, preserve strong predictive performance and produce informative uncertainty signals. The experiments also show that task quality, useful depth and internal stability are distinct properties.
  These results establish variational Transformers as a practical form of uncertainty-aware language modeling. They show that Transformers can predict with an explicit internal structure of uncertainty, which supports stronger probabilistic evaluation and a more informative analysis of model behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces variational neurons into Transformers' feed-forward computation to embed uncertainty into the internal layers, improving both probabilistic evaluation and model analysis.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitation of traditional Transformers, which mainly express uncertainty at the output layer, by embedding uncertainty internally for enhanced probabilistic analysis.
AI.method: The authors replace deterministic feed-forward units in Transformers with local variational units based on EVE, maintaining the original Transformer framework and verifying the approach through next-token language modeling experiments.
AI.result: Variational neurons integrate stably into Transformers, maintaining strong predictive performance, and offering better uncertainty signals for evaluation, without degrading model accuracy or calibration.
AI.conclusion: The study establishes that variational Transformers are practical for uncertainty-aware language modeling, enabling stronger probabilistic evaluation and providing insights into model behavior.

title: Falcon Perception
summary:
Perception-centric systems are typically implemented with a modular encoder-decoder pipeline: a vision backbone for feature extraction and a separate decoder (or late-fusion module) for task prediction. This raises a central question: is this architectural separation essential or can a single early-fusion stack do both perception and task modeling at scale? We introduce Falcon Perception, a unified dense Transformer that processes image patches and text tokens in a shared parameter space from the first layer, using a hybrid attention pattern (bidirectional among image tokens, causal for prediction tokens) to combine global visual context with autoregressive, variable-length instance generation. To keep dense outputs practical, Falcon Perception retains a lightweight token interface and decodes continuous spatial outputs with specialized heads, enabling parallel high-resolution mask prediction.
  Our design promotes simplicity: we keep a single scalable backbone and shift complexity toward data and training signals, adding only small heads where outputs are continuous and dense. On SA-Co, Falcon Perception improves mask quality to 68.0 Macro-F$_1$ compared to 62.3 of SAM3. We also introduce PBench, a benchmark targeting compositional prompts (OCR, spatial constraints, relations) and dense long-context regimes, where the model shows better gains. Finally, we extend the same early-fusion recipe to Falcon OCR: a compact 300M-parameter model which attains 80.3% on olmOCR and 88.64 on OmniDocBench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Falcon Perception, a unified Transformer-based system that integrates perception and task modeling into one architecture, achieving state-of-the-art results in dense prediction and OCR tasks.
AI.motivation: The architectural complexity of current modular encoder-decoder pipelines in perception-centric systems raises the question of whether a unified, early-fusion approach could simplify the design while achieving scalability and efficiency.
AI.method: The paper proposes Falcon Perception, a dense Transformer model that fuses image and text inputs from the first layer using a hybrid attention mechanism. This design uses specialized heads for dense spatial predictions while maintaining simplicity by focusing on data and training signals.
AI.result: Falcon Perception improves mask quality to 68.0 Macro-F$_1$ on SA-Co, outperforming SAM3’s 62.3. The model also demonstrates significant better performance in compositional tasks and long-context regimes on the PBench benchmark. Additionally, Falcon OCR, based on this approach, achieves high scores on olmOCR (80.3%) and OmniDocBench (88.64).
AI.conclusion: The unified early-fusion architecture of Falcon Perception proves to be effective in combining perception and task modeling, achieving both simplicity and strong performance across multiple benchmarks.

title: ResAdapt: Adaptive Resolution for Efficient Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve stronger visual understanding by scaling input fidelity, yet the resulting visual token growth makes jointly sustaining high spatial resolution and long temporal context prohibitive. We argue that the bottleneck lies not in how post-encoding representations are compressed but in the volume of pixels the encoder receives, and address it with ResAdapt, an Input-side adaptation framework that learns how much visual budget each frame should receive before encoding. ResAdapt couples a lightweight Allocator with an unchanged MLLM backbone, so the backbone retains its native visual-token interface while receiving an operator-transformed input. We formulate allocation as a contextual bandit and train the Allocator with Cost-Aware Policy Optimization (CAPO), which converts sparse rollout feedback into a stable accuracy-cost learning signal. Across budget-controlled video QA, temporal grounding, and image reasoning tasks, ResAdapt improves low-budget operating points and often lies on or near the efficiency-accuracy frontier, with the clearest gains on reasoning-intensive benchmarks under aggressive compression. Notably, ResAdapt supports up to 16x more frames at the same visual budget while delivering over 15% performance gain. Code is available at https://github.com/Xnhyacinth/ResAdapt.

AI.tldr: ResAdapt improves MLLMs' efficiency-accuracy balancing by adaptively allocating visual budgets to input frames before encoding, enabling better performance under resource constraints.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of maintaining high spatial resolution and long temporal context in MLLMs due to the growth in visual token volumes. It identifies the bottleneck at the input stage and targets efficient visual resource allocation.
AI.method: ResAdapt incorporates an Allocator that adjusts each frame's visual budget pre-encoding. It uses Cost-Aware Policy Optimization (CAPO) to train the Allocator, leveraging sparse rollout feedback for accuracy-cost optimization.
AI.result: ResAdapt significantly enhances performance under constrained visual budgets, improves reasoning-intensive tasks, and supports up to 16x more frames while delivering over 15% performance gain.
AI.conclusion: ResAdapt demonstrates its capability as an efficient input adaptation framework for MLLMs, pushing efficiency and accuracy boundaries, especially for compressed high-reasoning tasks.

title: Detecting the Unexpected: AI-Driven Anomaly Detection in Smart Bridge Monitoring
summary: Bridges are critical components of national infrastructure and smart cities. Therefore, smart bridge monitoring is essential for ensuring public safety and preventing catastrophic failures or accidents. Traditional bridge monitoring methods rely heavily on human visual inspections, which are time-consuming and prone to subjectivity and error. This paper proposes an artificial intelligence (AI)-driven anomaly detection approach for smart bridge monitoring. Specifically, a simple machine learning (ML) model is developed using real-time sensor data collected by the iBridge sensor devices installed on a bridge in Norway. The proposed model is evaluated against different ML models. Experimental results demonstrate that the density-based spatial clustering of applications with noise (DBSCAN)-based model outperforms in accurately detecting the anomalous events (bridge accident). These findings indicate that the proposed model is well-suited for smart bridge monitoring and can enhance public safety by enabling the timely detection of unforeseen incidents.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes an AI-driven anomaly detection model using real-time sensor data for smart bridge monitoring, demonstrating its superiority (via DBSCAN) in accurately detecting anomalies.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the inefficiencies and limitations in traditional human-based bridge monitoring methods, which are time-consuming, subjective, and error-prone, ensuring public safety and infrastructure reliability.
AI.method: A machine learning model, particularly focusing on the DBSCAN algorithm, is developed and evaluated using real-time sensor data from iBridge devices installed on a bridge in Norway, comparing its performance against various ML models.
AI.result: The DBSCAN-based model demonstrated superior accuracy in detecting anomalous bridge events, outperforming other machine learning models tested.
AI.conclusion: The proposed AI-driven model enhances the efficiency of smart bridge monitoring systems, offering a reliable and timely way to detect unforeseen incidents and improve public safety.

title: HMPDM: A Diffusion Model for Driving Video Prediction with Historical Motion Priors
summary: Video prediction is a useful function for autonomous driving, enabling intelligent vehicles to reliably anticipate how driving scenes will evolve and thereby supporting reasoning and safer planning. However, existing models are constrained by multi-stage training pipelines and remain insufficient in modeling the diverse motion patterns in real driving scenes, leading to degraded temporal consistency and visual quality. To address these challenges, this paper introduces the historical motion priors-informed diffusion model (HMPDM), a video prediction model that leverages historical motion priors to enhance motion understanding and temporal coherence. The proposed deep learning system introduces three key designs: (i) a Temporal-aware Latent Conditioning (TaLC) module for implicit historical motion injection; (ii) a Motion-aware Pyramid Encoder (MaPE) for multi-scale motion representation; (iii) a Self-Conditioning (SC) strategy for stable iterative denoising. Extensive experiments on the Cityscapes and KITTI benchmarks demonstrate that HMPDM outperforms state-of-the-art video prediction methods with efficiency, achieving a 28.2% improvement in FVD on Cityscapes under the same monocular RGB input configuration setting. The implementation codes are publicly available at https://github.com/KELISBU/HMPDM.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the HMPDM model for video prediction in autonomous driving, improving motion understanding and temporal coherence using three novel designs.
AI.motivation: Existing video prediction models face issues like degraded temporal consistency and insufficient modeling of motion diversity in driving scenes.
AI.method: HMPDM leverages historical motion priors through key designs: a TaLC module for historical motion injection, a MaPE module for multi-scale motion representation, and a SC strategy for stable denoising.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on Cityscapes and KITTI benchmarks show HMPDM achieves a 28.2% improvement in FVD on Cityscapes compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: HMPDM enhances video prediction for autonomous driving through innovative designs, boosting performance and visual quality.

title: MR-ImagenTime: Multi-Resolution Time Series Generation through Dual Image Representations
summary: Time series forecasting is vital across many domains, yet existing models struggle with fixed-length inputs and inadequate multi-scale modeling. We propose MR-CDM, a framework combining hierarchical multi-resolution trend decomposition, an adaptive embedding mechanism for variable-length inputs, and a multi-scale conditional diffusion process. Evaluations on four real-world datasets demonstrate that MR-CDM significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines (e.g., CSDI, Informer), reducing MAE and RMSE by approximately 6-10 to a certain degree.

AI.tldr: MR-CDM improves time series forecasting by addressing fixed-length inputs and multi-scale modeling limitations, achieving better results than existing methods.
AI.motivation: To improve time series forecasting by overcoming limitations of fixed-length inputs and inadequate multi-scale modeling in current approaches.
AI.method: The paper introduces MR-CDM, which uses hierarchical trend decomposition, adaptive embedding for variable-length inputs, and a multi-scale conditional diffusion model.
AI.result: MR-CDM outperformed state-of-the-art methods (e.g., CSDI, Informer) on four datasets, reducing prediction errors in MAE and RMSE by 6-10%.
AI.conclusion: MR-CDM offers a robust approach for time series forecasting, enhancing predictive accuracy and adaptability compared to competing frameworks.

title: Bridging Visual Representation and Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards in Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has substantially enhanced the reasoning capabilities of large language models in abstract reasoning tasks. However, its application to Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) remains constrained by a structural representational bottleneck. Existing approaches generally lack explicit modeling and effective utilization of visual information, preventing visual representations from being tightly coupled with the reinforcement learning optimization process and thereby limiting further improvements in multimodal reasoning performance. To address this limitation, we propose KAWHI (Key-Region Aligned Weighted Harmonic Incentive), a plug-and-play reward reweighting mechanism that explicitly incorporates structured visual information into uniform reward policy optimization methods (e.g., GRPO and GSPO). The method adaptively localizes semantically salient regions through hierarchical geometric aggregation, identifies vision-critical attention heads via structured attribution, and performs paragraph-level credit reallocation to align spatial visual evidence with semantically decisive reasoning steps. Extensive empirical evaluations on diverse reasoning benchmarks substantiate KAWHI as a general-purpose enhancement module, consistently improving the performance of various uniform reward optimization methods. Project page: KAWHI (https://kawhiiiileo.github.io/KAWHI_PAGE/)

AI.tldr: KAWHI improves multimodal reasoning by integrating structured visual information within reinforcement learning-based reward optimization.
AI.motivation: Overcome limitations in LVLMs reasoning performance by leveraging visual information effectively in RLVR.
AI.method: Develop KAWHI, a reward reweighting mechanism that links visual regions to reasoning steps via structured attribution and paragraph-level credit reallocation.
AI.result: Demonstrated performance improvements across various reasoning benchmarks with KAWHI integrated into existing methods.
AI.conclusion: KAWHI proves to be a broadly applicable enhancement module for reinforcing coupling between vision and reasoning tasks.

title: Decompose, Mix, Adapt: A Unified Framework for Parameter-Efficient Neural Network Recombination and Compression
summary: Parameter Recombination (PR) methods aim to efficiently compose the weights of a neural network for applications like Parameter-Efficient FineTuning (PEFT) and Model Compression (MC), among others. Most methods typically focus on one application of PR, which can make composing them challenging. For example, when deploying a large model you may wish to compress the model and also quickly adapt to new settings. However, PEFT methods often can still contain millions of parameters. This may be small compared to the original model size, but can be problematic in resource constrained deployments like edge devices, where they take a larger portion of the compressed model's parameters. To address this, we present Coefficient-gated weight Recombination by Interpolated Shared basis Projections (CRISP), a general approach that seamlessly integrates multiple PR tasks within the same framework. CRISP accomplishes this by factorizing pretrained weights into basis matrices and their component mixing projections. Sharing basis matrices across layers and adjusting its size enables us to perform MC, whereas the mixer weight's small size (fewer than 200 in some experiments) enables CRISP to support PEFT. Experiments show CRISP outperforms methods from prior work capable of dual-task applications by 4-5\% while also outperforming the state-of-the-art in PEFT by 1.5\% and PEFT+MC combinations by 1\%. Our code is available on the repository: https://github.com/appledora/CRISP-CVPR26.

AI.tldr: CRISP introduces a new PR method to integrate Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning and Model Compression within a single framework, enabling better performance across tasks and reducing parameter overhead.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address inefficiencies and limitations in current Parameter Recombination methods, especially for edge deployments requiring simultaneous Model Compression and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning.
AI.method: CRISP uses pretrained weight factorization into basis matrices and component projections and enables sharing and adjusting basis matrices across layers for compression, while using small mixer weights for fine-tuning.
AI.result: CRISP outperforms prior dual-task PR methods by 4-5%, state-of-the-art PEFT methods by 1.5%, and PEFT+MC combinations by 1%.
AI.conclusion: CRISP effectively bridges multiple PR tasks, offering a unified and resource-efficient framework for applications such as PEFT and Model Compression, making it suitable for constrained environments like edge devices.

title: ParaSpeechCLAP: A Dual-Encoder Speech-Text Model for Rich Stylistic Language-Audio Pretraining
summary: We introduce ParaSpeechCLAP, a dual-encoder contrastive model that maps speech and text style captions into a common embedding space, supporting a wide range of intrinsic (speaker-level) and situational (utterance-level) descriptors (such as pitch, texture and emotion) far beyond the narrow set handled by existing models. We train specialized ParaSpeechCLAP-Intrinsic and ParaSpeechCLAP-Situational models alongside a unified ParaSpeechCLAP-Combined model, finding that specialization yields stronger performance on individual style dimensions while the unified model excels on compositional evaluation. We further show that ParaSpeechCLAP-Intrinsic benefits from an additional classification loss and class-balanced training. We demonstrate our models' performance on style caption retrieval, speech attribute classification and as an inference-time reward model that improves style-prompted TTS without additional training. ParaSpeechCLAP outperforms baselines on most metrics across all three applications. Our models and code are released at https://github.com/ajd12342/paraspeechclap .

AI.tldr: This paper presents ParaSpeechCLAP, a model that aligns speech and text style descriptions in a shared embedding space, showcasing its superior performance in style caption retrieval, speech classification, and style-informed TTS.
AI.motivation: Existing models are limited in handling diverse intrinsic and situational style descriptors for speech. The authors aim to create a model that can handle a wider set of style descriptors.
AI.method: The authors developed ParaSpeechCLAP by training dual-encoder contrastive models focusing on intrinsic, situational, and combined descriptors. They evaluated specialization vs. unification, and enhanced Intrinsic model training with classification loss and balanced data.
AI.result: ParaSpeechCLAP outperformed baseline models in style caption retrieval, speech classification, and style-prompted TTS tasks, showing stronger performance in specialized models and versatility in the unified model.
AI.conclusion: This study establishes ParaSpeechCLAP as a state-of-the-art system for speech and text style alignment, providing a robust tool for multiple speech and text applications. Models and code are publicly available.

title: Corruption-robust Offline Multi-agent Reinforcement Learning From Human Feedback
summary: We consider robustness against data corruption in offline multi-agent reinforcement learning from human feedback (MARLHF) under a strong-contamination model: given a dataset $D$ of trajectory-preference tuples (each preference being an $n$-dimensional binary label vector representing each of the $n$ agents' preferences), an $ε$-fraction of the samples may be arbitrarily corrupted. We model the problem using the framework of linear Markov games. First, under a uniform coverage assumption - where every policy of interest is sufficiently represented in the clean (prior to corruption) data - we introduce a robust estimator that guarantees an $O(ε^{1 - o(1)})$ bound on the Nash equilibrium gap. Next, we move to the more challenging unilateral coverage setting, in which only a Nash equilibrium and its single-player deviations are covered. In this case, our proposed algorithm achieves an $O(\sqrtε)$ bound on the Nash gap. Both of these procedures, however, suffer from intractable computation. To address this, we relax our solution concept to coarse correlated equilibria (CCE). Under the same unilateral coverage regime, we derive a quasi-polynomial-time algorithm whose CCE gap scales as $O(\sqrtε)$. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first systematic treatment of adversarial data corruption in offline MARLHF.

AI.tldr: This paper studies the problem of adversarial data corruption in offline multi-agent reinforcement learning from human feedback (MARLHF). It proposes robust estimators and algorithms with performance guarantees under different data coverage assumptions and computational constraints.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the challenge of learning reliable policies despite the presence of adversarial corruption in the data, which is a crucial aspect for ensuring real-world applicability and resilience of MARLHF systems.
AI.method: The paper develops strategies under two coverage regimes: uniform and unilateral, introducing robust estimators for the Nash equilibrium gap. Due to computational challenges, it also proposes a relaxed equilibrium concept (coarse correlated equilibria) and derives a quasi-polynomial-time algorithm for achieving robustness.
AI.result: The robust estimators guarantee $O(ε^{1 - o(1)})$ and $O(√ε)$ bounds in the uniform and unilateral coverage cases respectively. The quasi-polynomial-time algorithm achieves $O(√ε)$ bounds for coarse correlated equilibria under unilateral coverage.
AI.conclusion: The study provides a systematic framework for handling adversarial corruption in offline MARLHF, offering both theoretical guarantees and practical solutions. However, computational intractability at exact Nash equilibria remains a limitation.

title: Mind the Shape Gap: A Benchmark and Baseline for Deformation-Aware 6D Pose Estimation of Agricultural Produce
summary: Accurate 6D pose estimation for robotic harvesting is fundamentally hindered by the biological deformability and high intra-class shape variability of agricultural produce. Instance-level methods fail in this setting, as obtaining exact 3D models for every unique piece of produce is practically infeasible, while category-level approaches that rely on a fixed template suffer significant accuracy degradation when the prior deviates from the true instance geometry. To bridge such lack of robustness to deformation, we introduce PEAR (Pose and dEformation of Agricultural pRoduce), the first benchmark providing joint 6D pose and per-instance 3D deformation ground truth across 8 produce categories, acquired via a robotic manipulator for high annotation accuracy. Using PEAR, we show that state-of-the-art methods suffer up to 6x performance degradation when faced with the inherent geometric deviations of real-world produce. Motivated by this finding, we propose SEED (Simultaneous Estimation of posE and Deformation), a unified RGB-only framework that jointly predicts 6D pose and explicit lattice deformations from a single image across multiple produce categories. Trained entirely on synthetic data with generative texture augmentation applied at the UV level, SEED outperforms MegaPose on 6 out of 8 categories under identical RGB-only conditions, demonstrating that explicit shape modeling is a critical step toward reliable pose estimation in agricultural robotics.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses challenges in 6D pose estimation for agricultural produce, introducing a new benchmark (PEAR) and a novel method (SEED) for improved robustness against geometric deformation.
AI.motivation: Robotic harvesting requires precise 6D pose estimation, but current methods struggle due to the deformable and variable shapes of agricultural produce.
AI.method: The authors create PEAR, a benchmark with detailed pose and deformation data, and propose SEED, an RGB-only framework that predicts pose and shape deformation using synthetic data and texture augmentation.
AI.result: SEED outperforms a baseline method (MegaPose) on most categories, showing better performance for deformable produce under RGB-only settings.
AI.conclusion: Explicit modeling of deformation significantly improves the reliability of 6D pose estimation in agricultural robotics.

title: SpatialStack: Layered Geometry-Language Fusion for 3D VLM Spatial Reasoning
summary: Large vision-language models (VLMs) still struggle with reliable 3D spatial reasoning, a core capability for embodied and physical AI systems. This limitation arises from their inability to capture fine-grained 3D geometry and spatial relationships. While recent efforts have introduced multi-view geometry transformers into VLMs, they typically fuse only the deep-layer features from vision and geometry encoders, discarding rich hierarchical signals and creating a fundamental bottleneck for spatial understanding. To overcome this, we propose SpatialStack, a general hierarchical fusion framework that progressively aligns vision, geometry, and language representations across the model hierarchy. Moving beyond conventional late-stage vision-geometry fusion, SpatialStack stacks and synchronizes multi-level geometric features with the language backbone, enabling the model to capture both local geometric precision and global contextual semantics. Building upon this framework, we develop VLM-SpatialStack, a model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple 3D spatial reasoning benchmarks. Extensive experiments and ablations demonstrate that our multi-level fusion strategy consistently enhances 3D understanding and generalizes robustly across diverse spatial reasoning tasks, establishing SpatialStack as an effective and extensible design paradigm for vision-language-geometry integration in next-generation multimodal physical AI systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SpatialStack, a hierarchical fusion framework for better 3D spatial reasoning in vision-language models (VLMs), achieving state-of-the-art results in various benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Large vision-language models (VLMs) struggle with accurate 3D spatial reasoning due to poor capturing of fine-grained 3D geometry and spatial relationships.
AI.method: The authors introduce SpatialStack, a hierarchical fusion framework that integrates vision, geometry, and language representations across multiple levels instead of limiting fusion to deep-layer features. This approach synchronizes multi-level geometric features with the language backbone.
AI.result: The proposed VLM-SpatialStack model achieves state-of-the-art performance on several 3D spatial reasoning benchmarks, demonstrating improved 3D understanding and robustness.
AI.conclusion: SpatialStack proves to be an effective and extensible framework for enhancing vision-language-geometry integration, benefiting next-generation multimodal physical AI systems.

title: FI-KAN: Fractal Interpolation Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
comment: 37 pages, 20 figures, 14 tables. Code available at: https://github.com/ReFractals/fractal-interpolation-kan
summary: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) employ B-spline bases on a fixed grid, providing no intrinsic multi-scale decomposition for non-smooth function approximation. We introduce Fractal Interpolation KAN (FI-KAN), which incorporates learnable fractal interpolation function (FIF) bases from iterated function system (IFS) theory into KAN. Two variants are presented: Pure FI-KAN (Barnsley, 1986) replaces B-splines entirely with FIF bases; Hybrid FI-KAN (Navascues, 2005) retains the B-spline path and adds a learnable fractal correction. The IFS contraction parameters give each edge a differentiable fractal dimension that adapts to target regularity during training. On a Holder regularity benchmark ($α\in [0.2, 2.0]$), Hybrid FI-KAN outperforms KAN at every regularity level (1.3x to 33x). On fractal targets, FI-KAN achieves up to 6.3x MSE reduction over KAN, maintaining 4.7x advantage at 5 dB SNR. On non-smooth PDE solutions (scikit-fem), Hybrid FI-KAN achieves up to 79x improvement on rough-coefficient diffusion and 3.5x on L-shaped domain corner singularities. Pure FI-KAN's complementary behavior, dominating on rough targets while underperforming on smooth ones, provides controlled evidence that basis geometry must match target regularity. A fractal dimension regularizer provides interpretable complexity control whose learned values recover the true fractal dimension of each target. These results establish regularity-matched basis design as a principled strategy for neural function approximation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Fractal Interpolation KAN (FI-KAN), enhancing Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks with fractal interpolation bases for improved function approximation across varying regularities and targets.
AI.motivation: KAN's reliance on fixed B-spline bases lacks multi-scale adaptability for non-smooth functions, prompting the need for bases tailored to target regularity.
AI.method: Two FI-KAN variants are proposed: Pure FI-KAN (using fractal interpolation bases entirely) and Hybrid FI-KAN (combining B-splines with learnable fractal corrections). Training adapts fractal dimensions to target regularity, with regularization to control complexity.
AI.result: Hybrid FI-KAN consistently outperforms KAN across benchmarks, achieving significant error reductions on fractal and non-smooth PDE datasets. Pure FI-KAN excels in approximating rough targets but underperforms on smoother ones.
AI.conclusion: Fractal dimension regularization and geometry-matching provide robust strategies for neural approximations, highlighting FI-KAN's effectiveness and principled basis design.

title: Evaluating Large and Lightweight Vision Models for Irregular Component Segmentation in E-Waste Disassembly
summary: Precise segmentation of irregular and densely arranged components is essential for robotic disassembly and material recovery in electronic waste (e-waste) recycling. This study evaluates the impact of model architecture and scale on segmentation performance by comparing SAM2, a transformer-based vision model, with the lightweight YOLOv8 network. Both models were trained and tested on a newly collected dataset of 1,456 annotated RGB images of laptop components including logic boards, heat sinks, and fans, captured under varying illumination and orientation conditions. Data augmentation techniques, such as random rotation, flipping, and cropping, were applied to improve model robustness. YOLOv8 achieved higher segmentation accuracy (mAP50 = 98.8%, mAP50-95 = 85%) and stronger boundary precision than SAM2 (mAP50 = 8.4%). SAM2 demonstrated flexibility in representing diverse object structures but often produced overlapping masks and inconsistent contours. These findings show that large pre-trained models require task-specific optimization for industrial applications. The resulting dataset and benchmarking framework provide a foundation for developing scalable vision algorithms for robotic e-waste disassembly and circular manufacturing systems.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates the segmentation performance of two vision models, SAM2 and YOLOv8, on a dataset of e-waste laptop components. YOLOv8 outperformed SAM2 in accuracy and boundary precision.
AI.motivation: To enable precise segmentation of irregular and densely packed components in robotic e-waste disassembly, facilitating material recovery for recycling.
AI.method: The study compared the transformer-based SAM2 model with the YOLOv8 network using a dataset of 1,456 annotated RGB images of laptop components. Data augmentation techniques were employed to enhance model performance.
AI.result: YOLOv8 achieved significantly better segmentation accuracy (mAP50 = 98.8%, mAP50-95 = 85%) and boundary precision compared to SAM2 (mAP50 = 8.4%). SAM2 exhibited flexibility but suffered from overlapping masks and inconsistent contours.
AI.conclusion: Large pre-trained models like SAM2 need task-specific optimization for industrial applications. The dataset and benchmarking framework developed in this study offer a foundation for scalable vision algorithms in robotic e-waste recycling.

title: LOME: Learning Human-Object Manipulation with Action-Conditioned Egocentric World Model
summary: Learning human-object manipulation presents significant challenges due to its fine-grained and contact-rich nature of the motions involved. Traditional physics-based animation requires extensive modeling and manual setup, and more importantly, it neither generalizes well across diverse object morphologies nor scales effectively to real-world environment. To address these limitations, we introduce LOME, an egocentric world model that can generate realistic human-object interactions as videos conditioned on an input image, a text prompt, and per-frame human actions, including both body poses and hand gestures. LOME injects strong and precise action guidance into object manipulation by jointly estimating spatial human actions and the environment contexts during training. After finetuning a pretrained video generative model on videos of diverse egocentric human-object interactions, LOME demonstrates not only high action-following accuracy and strong generalization to unseen scenarios, but also realistic physical consequences of hand-object interactions, e.g., liquid flowing from a bottle into a mug after executing a ``pouring'' action. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our video-based framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art image based and video-based action-conditioned methods and Image/Text-to-Video (I/T2V) generative model in terms of both temporal consistency and motion control. LOME paves the way for photorealistic AR/VR experiences and scalable robotic training, without being limited to simulated environments or relying on explicit 3D/4D modeling.

AI.tldr: LOME is a video-based framework capable of generating realistic human-object interaction videos based on input image, text prompt, and per-frame human actions.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for modeling human-object manipulation are limited in terms of generalizability, scalability, and realism, necessitating a novel approach.
AI.method: LOME integrates egocentric world modeling with a pretrained video generative model, finetuned using diverse human-object interaction videos, incorporating spatial and contextual training for precise action guidance.
AI.result: The framework achieves high accuracy in action-following, generalizes well to unseen scenarios, and produces realistic physical consequences in human-object interactions.
AI.conclusion: LOME outperforms state-of-the-art methods in generating temporally consistent and controllable video outputs, facilitating advancements in AR/VR and robotic training.

title: NeiGAD: Augmenting Graph Anomaly Detection via Spectral Neighbor Information
summary: Graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify irregular nodes or structures in attributed graphs. Neighbor information, which reflects both structural connectivity and attribute consistency with surrounding nodes, is essential for distinguishing anomalies from normal patterns. Although recent graph neural network (GNN)-based methods incorporate such information through message passing, they often fail to explicitly model its effect or interaction with attributes, limiting detection performance. This work introduces NeiGAD, a novel plug-and-play module that captures neighbor information through spectral graph analysis. Theoretical insights demonstrate that eigenvectors of the adjacency matrix encode local neighbor interactions and progressively amplify anomaly signals. Based on this, NeiGAD selects a compact set of eigenvectors to construct efficient and discriminative representations. Experiments on eight real-world datasets show that NeiGAD consistently improves detection accuracy and outperforms state-of-the-art GAD methods. These results demonstrate the importance of explicit neighbor modeling and the effectiveness of spectral analysis in anomaly detection. Code is available at: https://github.com/huafeihuang/NeiGAD.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces NeiGAD, a module for detecting irregularities in graphs using spectral graph analysis to model neighbor information, improving detection accuracy over existing methods.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to improve graph anomaly detection by explicitly modeling neighbor information, as current methods struggle with this aspect, which limits their performance.
AI.method: NeiGAD uses a spectral graph analysis approach, leveraging eigenvectors of the adjacency matrix to capture local neighbor interactions and amplify anomaly signals, constructing compact and discriminative representations.
AI.result: Experiments on eight real-world datasets show that NeiGAD improves detection accuracy and outperforms state-of-the-art GAD methods.
AI.conclusion: The study emphasizes the importance of explicit neighbor modeling and highlights the effectiveness of spectral analysis for improving anomaly detection in graphs.

title: From None to All: Self-Supervised 3D Reconstruction via Novel View Synthesis
summary: In this paper, we introduce NAS3R, a self-supervised feed-forward framework that jointly learns explicit 3D geometry and camera parameters with no ground-truth annotations and no pretrained priors. During training, NAS3R reconstructs 3D Gaussians from uncalibrated and unposed context views and renders target views using its self-predicted camera parameters, enabling self-supervised training from 2D photometric supervision. To ensure stable convergence, NAS3R integrates reconstruction and camera prediction within a shared transformer backbone regulated by masked attention, and adopts a depth-based Gaussian formulation that facilitates well-conditioned optimization. The framework is compatible with state-of-the-art supervised 3D reconstruction architectures and can incorporate pretrained priors or intrinsic information when available. Extensive experiments show that NAS3R achieves superior results to other self-supervised methods, establishing a scalable and geometry-aware paradigm for 3D reconstruction from unconstrained data. Code and models are publicly available at https://ranrhuang.github.io/nas3r/.

AI.tldr: NAS3R introduces a self-supervised framework for learning 3D geometry and camera parameters without requiring ground-truth annotations or pretrained priors.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of 3D reconstruction from unconstrained data using self-supervision, eliminating the need for annotations or pretraining while achieving scalable and geometry-aware optimization.
AI.method: NAS3R utilizes a shared transformer backbone with masked attention and a depth-based Gaussian formulation. It jointly learns geometry and camera parameters through self-supervised 2D photometric training on uncalibrated views.
AI.result: NAS3R outperforms existing self-supervised methods in terms of 3D reconstruction quality and scalability, and it is compatible with both supervised models and pretrained priors.
AI.conclusion: The framework establishes a robust, scalable paradigm for geometry-aware 3D reconstruction, demonstrating strong performance even without external annotations or priors.

title: LIBERO-Para: A Diagnostic Benchmark and Metrics for Paraphrase Robustness in VLA Models
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models achieve strong performance in robotic manipulation by leveraging pre-trained vision-language backbones. However, in downstream robotic settings, they are typically fine-tuned with limited data, leading to overfitting to specific instruction formulations and leaving robustness to paraphrased instructions underexplored. To study this gap, we introduce LIBERO-Para, a controlled benchmark that independently varies action expressions and object references for fine-grained analysis of linguistic generalization. Across seven VLA configurations (0.6B-7.5B), we observe consistent performance degradation of 22-52 pp under paraphrasing. This degradation is primarily driven by object-level lexical variation: even simple synonym substitutions cause large drops, indicating reliance on surface-level matching rather than semantic grounding. Moreover, 80-96% of failures arise from planning-level trajectory divergence rather than execution errors, showing that paraphrasing disrupts task identification. Binary success rate treats all paraphrases equally, obscuring whether models perform consistently across difficulty levels or rely on easier cases. To address this, we propose PRIDE, a metric that quantifies paraphrase difficulty using semantic and syntactic factors. Our benchmark and corresponding code are available at: https://github.com/cau-hai-lab/LIBERO-Para

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates Vision-Language-Action models in robotic manipulation tasks with a focus on linguistic robustness, introducing LIBERO-Para to explore failures arising from paraphrased instructions.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the lack of robustness in Vision-Language-Action models when faced with paraphrased instructions, as current models tend to overfit with limited fine-tuning data.
AI.method: LIBERO-Para benchmark is introduced to analyze the linguistic generalization of VLA models, varying action expressions and object references. The PRIDE metric is proposed to quantify paraphrase difficulty.
AI.result: Tests show significant performance degradation (22-52 percentage points) in VLA models under paraphrasing, primarily driven by lexical variations. Planning-level failures constitute 80-96% of errors.
AI.conclusion: Paraphrasing disrupts task identification in VLA models, highlighting their reliance on surface-level matching rather than semantic understanding. The study calls for enhanced linguistic grounding in these models.

title: Project Imaging-X: A Survey of 1000+ Open-Access Medical Imaging Datasets for Foundation Model Development
summary: Foundation models have demonstrated remarkable success across diverse domains and tasks, primarily due to the thrive of large-scale, diverse, and high-quality datasets. However, in the field of medical imaging, the curation and assembling of such medical datasets are highly challenging due to the reliance on clinical expertise and strict ethical and privacy constraints, resulting in a scarcity of large-scale unified medical datasets and hindering the development of powerful medical foundation models. In this work, we present the largest survey to date of medical image datasets, covering over 1,000 open-access datasets with a systematic catalog of their modalities, tasks, anatomies, annotations, limitations, and potential for integration. Our analysis exposes a landscape that is modest in scale, fragmented across narrowly scoped tasks, and unevenly distributed across organs and modalities, which in turn limits the utility of existing medical image datasets for developing versatile and robust medical foundation models. To turn fragmentation into scale, we propose a metadata-driven fusion paradigm (MDFP) that integrates public datasets with shared modalities or tasks, thereby transforming multiple small data silos into larger, more coherent resources. Building on MDFP, we release an interactive discovery portal that enables end-to-end, automated medical image dataset integration, and compile all surveyed datasets into a unified, structured table that clearly summarizes their key characteristics and provides reference links, offering the community an accessible and comprehensive repository. By charting the current terrain and offering a principled path to dataset consolidation, our survey provides a practical roadmap for scaling medical imaging corpora, supporting faster data discovery, more principled dataset creation, and more capable medical foundation models.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys over 1,000 medical image datasets to address issues of fragmentation and scalability, proposing a metadata-driven fusion paradigm (MDFP) for dataset integration and releasing tools for streamlined, unified medical image resource development.
AI.motivation: Medical imaging faces challenges in creating large-scale, diverse datasets due to reliance on clinical expertise and privacy concerns, limiting the progress of robust medical foundation models.
AI.method: The paper systematically categorizes existing open-access medical image datasets, identifies fragmentation issues, and introduces a metadata-driven fusion paradigm (MDFP) to consolidate smaller datasets.
AI.result: The authors provide a unified, accessible repository of over 1,000 datasets and an interactive discovery portal, demonstrating the benefits of dataset integration through MDFP.
AI.conclusion: This work offers a roadmap to overcome fragmentation in medical imaging datasets, facilitating faster data discovery and supporting future development of medical foundation models.

title: HASS: Hierarchical Simulation of Logopenic Aphasic Speech for Scalable PPA Detection
summary: Building a diagnosis model for primary progressive aphasia (PPA) has been challenging due to the data scarcity. Collecting clinical data at scale is limited by the high vulnerability of clinical population and the high cost of expert labeling. To circumvent this, previous studies simulate dysfluent speech to generate training data. However, those approaches are not comprehensive enough to simulate PPA as holistic, multi-level phenotypes, instead relying on isolated dysfluencies. To address this, we propose a novel, clinically grounded simulation framework, Hierarchical Aphasic Speech Simulation (HASS). HASS aims to simulate behaviors of logopenic variant of PPA (lvPPA) with varying degrees of severity. To this end, semantic, phonological, and temporal deficits of lvPPA are systematically identified by clinical experts, and simulated. We demonstrate that our framework enables more accurate and generalizable detection models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new framework, HASS, to simulate speech deficits in primary progressive aphasia (PPA) and improve diagnosis models.
AI.motivation: Building diagnosis models for PPA faces challenges such as data scarcity and limitations in collecting expert-labeled clinical data.
AI.method: The proposed HASS framework simulates lvPPA speech deficits by systematically identifying and replicating semantic, phonological, and temporal deficits with varying severities, guided by clinical expertise.
AI.result: HASS enables the creation of more accurate and generalizable detection models for lvPPA.
AI.conclusion: HASS provides a clinically grounded, multi-level approach to simulate PPA phenotypes, addressing limitations in previous methods reliant on isolated dysfluencies.

title: Taming the Instability: A Robust Second-Order Optimizer for Federated Learning over Non-IID Data
summary: In this paper, we present Federated Robust Curvature Optimization (FedRCO), a novel second-order optimization framework designed to improve convergence speed and reduce communication cost in Federated Learning systems under statistical heterogeneity. Existing second-order optimization methods are often computationally expensive and numerically unstable in distributed settings. In contrast, FedRCO addresses these challenges by integrating an efficient approximate curvature optimizer with a provable stability mechanism. Specifically, FedRCO incorporates three key components: (1) a Gradient Anomaly Monitor that detects and mitigates exploding gradients in real-time, (2) a Fail-Safe Resilience protocol that resets optimization states upon numerical instability, and (3) a Curvature-Preserving Adaptive Aggregation strategy that safely integrates global knowledge without erasing the local curvature geometry. Theoretical analysis shows that FedRCO can effectively mitigate instability and prevent unbounded updates while preserving optimization efficiency. Extensive experiments show that FedRCO achieves superior robustness against diverse non-IID scenarios while achieving higher accuracy and faster convergence than both state-of-the-art first-order and second-order methods.

AI.tldr: FedRCO is a second-order optimization framework for Federated Learning, designed to enhance robustness, convergence, and communication efficiency under heterogeneous conditions.
AI.motivation: Existing optimizers in Federated Learning face challenges like high computational expense, numerical instability, and inefficiency under statistical heterogeneity.
AI.method: FedRCO employs three mechanisms: Gradient Anomaly Monitor to manage exploding gradients, Fail-Safe Resilience for stability resets, and Curvature-Preserving Adaptive Aggregation for combining local and global knowledge.
AI.result: Theoretical and empirical findings demonstrate FedRCO's robust performance and faster convergence in diverse non-IID settings, surpassing first-order methods.
AI.conclusion: FedRCO provides a stable, efficient, and robust optimization solution for Federated Learning, advancing its applicability in real-world heterogeneous environments.

title: Difference Feedback: Generating Multimodal Process-Level Supervision for VLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Vision--language models (VLMs) are increasingly aligned via Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)-style training. However, relying solely on terminal outcome rewards yields sparse credit assignment in multi-step reasoning, weakening the linkage between visual evidence and intermediate steps and often causing unstable optimization and visual hallucinations. We propose Differential Feedback, which automatically constructs token/step-level supervision masks by repairing erroneous reasoning trajectories, explicitly marking the key positions that require correction. Without costly large-scale step-by-step human annotations, our method enables process-level visual alignment and can be seamlessly integrated into existing GRPO-like frameworks. Experiments on multimodal reasoning benchmarks including MMMStar and MathVista show an average 3% improvement under matched compute budgets. Our approach offers an effective, low-cost solution for accurate vision--reasoning process alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a method called Differential Feedback to improve vision--language models by offering token-level supervision through trajectory corrections, enhancing reasoning accuracy without requiring extensive human annotations.
AI.motivation: Despite the success of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) training in vision--language models, it suffers from sparse credit assignment and unstable optimization during multi-step reasoning tasks, leading to issues such as visual hallucinations.
AI.method: The proposed approach, Differential Feedback, identifies and corrects reasoning trajectory errors by automatically generating supervision masks at the token and step levels, enabling better alignment of intermediate reasoning steps without relying on large human-annotated datasets.
AI.result: The method achieves an average 3% improvement in performance on multimodal reasoning benchmarks like MMMStar and MathVista, under comparable compute budgets.
AI.conclusion: Differential Feedback provides a cost-effective way to enhance process-level visual reasoning alignment, making it a promising addition to existing GRPO-style frameworks for vision--language models.

title: FairGC: Fairness-aware Graph Condensation
summary: Graph condensation (GC) has become a vital strategy for scaling Graph Neural Networks by compressing massive datasets into small, synthetic node sets. While current GC methods effectively maintain predictive accuracy, they are primarily designed for utility and often ignore fairness constraints. Because these techniques are bias-blind, they frequently capture and even amplify demographic disparities found in the original data. This leads to synthetic proxies that are unsuitable for sensitive applications like credit scoring or social recommendations. To solve this problem, we introduce FairGC, a unified framework that embeds fairness directly into the graph distillation process. Our approach consists of three key components. First, a Distribution-Preserving Condensation module synchronizes the joint distributions of labels and sensitive attributes to stop bias from spreading. Second, a Spectral Encoding module uses Laplacian eigen-decomposition to preserve essential global structural patterns. Finally, a Fairness-Enhanced Neural Architecture employs multi-domain fusion and a label-smoothing curriculum to produce equitable predictions. Rigorous evaluations on four real-world datasets, show that FairGC provides a superior balance between accuracy and fairness. Our results confirm that FairGC significantly reduces disparity in Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity compared to existing state-of-the-art condensation models. The codes are available at https://github.com/LuoRenqiang/FairGC.

AI.tldr: FairGC is a framework designed to incorporate fairness into graph condensation, ensuring equitable predictions while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing Graph Condensation methods disregard fairness constraints, amplifying demographic biases and rendering them unsuitable for sensitive applications.
AI.method: FairGC integrates three components: a distribution-preserving condensation module, spectral encoding for structural pattern preservation, and a fairness-enhanced neural architecture.
AI.result: FairGC demonstrates superior accuracy and fairness on four datasets, reducing disparities in Statistical Parity and Equal Opportunity.
AI.conclusion: FairGC effectively embeds fairness in graph distillation, balancing predictive performance and equity in sensitive applications.

title: Estimating the Impact of COVID-19 on Travel Demand in Houston Area Using Deep Learning and Satellite Imagery
summary: Considering recent advances in remote sensing satellite systems and computer vision algorithms, many satellite sensing platforms and sensors have been used to monitor the condition and usage of transportation infrastructure systems. The level of details that can be detected increases significantly with the increase of ground sample distance (GSD), which is around 15 cm - 30 cm for high-resolution satellite images. In this study, we analyzed data acquired from high-resolution satellite imagery to provide insights, predictive signals, and trend for travel demand estimation. More specifically, we estimate the impact of COVID-19 in the metropolitan area of Houston using satellite imagery from Google Earth Engine datasets. We developed a car-counting model through Detectron2 and Faster R-CNN to monitor the presence of cars within different locations (i.e., university, shopping mall, community plaza, restaurant, supermarket) before and during the COVID-19. The results show that the number of cars detected at these selected locations reduced on average 30% in 2020 compared with the previous year 2019. The results also show that satellite imagery provides rich information for travel demand and economic activity estimation. Together with advanced computer vision and deep learning algorithms, it can generate reliable and accurate information for transportation agency decision makers.

AI.tldr: This paper utilizes high-resolution satellite imagery and computer vision algorithms to estimate the impact of COVID-19 on travel demand in Houston, focusing on car counting data.
AI.motivation: The authors aim to explore how satellite imagery, combined with advanced computer vision, can aid in monitoring transportation infrastructure and estimating travel and economic patterns, particularly during disruptions like COVID-19.
AI.method: The study analyzes car presence at various locations using high-resolution satellite data while employing the Detectron2 and Faster R-CNN models to count cars and extract patterns.
AI.result: The study found that the number of detected cars at selected locations dropped by an average of 30% during 2020 (COVID-19) compared to 2019.
AI.conclusion: Satellite imagery and deep learning algorithms together provide reliable insights into travel demand and economic activities, beneficial for decision-makers in transportation agencies.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Predicting Hydrogen Sorption in Geological Formations: Thermodynamically Constrained Deep Learning Integrating Classical Adsorption Theory
summary: Accurate prediction of hydrogen sorption in fine-grained geological materials is essential for evaluating underground hydrogen storage capacity, assessing caprock integrity, and characterizing hydrogen migration in subsurface energy systems. Classical isotherm models perform well at the individual-sample level but fail when generalized across heterogeneous populations, with the coefficient of determination collapsing from 0.80-0.90 for single-sample fits to 0.09-0.38 for aggregated multi-sample datasets. We present a multi-scale physics-informed neural network framework that addresses this limitation by embedding classical adsorption theory and thermodynamic constraints directly into the learning process. The framework utilizes 1,987 hydrogen sorption isotherm measurements across clays, shales, coals, supplemented by 224 characteristic uptake measurements. A seven-category physics-informed feature engineering scheme generates 62 thermodynamically meaningful descriptors from raw material characterization data. The loss function enforces saturation limits, a monotonic pressure response, and Van't Hoff temperature dependence via penalty weighting, while a three-phase curriculum-based training strategy ensures stable integration of competing physical constraints. An architecture-diverse ensemble of ten members provides calibrated uncertainty quantification, with post-hoc temperature scaling achieving target prediction interval coverage. The optimized PINN achieves R2 = 0.9544, RMSE = 0.0484 mmol/g, and MAE = 0.0231 mmol/g on the held-out test set, with 98.6% monotonicity satisfaction and zero non-physical negative predictions. Physics-informed regularization yields a 10-15% cross-lithology generalization advantage over a well-tuned random forest under leave-one-lithology-out validation, confirming that thermodynamic constraints transfer meaningfully across geological boundaries.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a physics-informed neural network (PINN) approach to accurately predict hydrogen sorption in geological materials, addressing limitations in generalizing classical isotherm models.
AI.motivation: The need for accurate predictions of hydrogen sorption in geological materials is critical for applications like hydrogen storage, caprock integrity evaluation, and subsurface energy system design. Classical models fail to generalize across diverse datasets, motivating the pursuit of a machine learning solution.
AI.method: The paper proposes a multi-scale PINN that integrates classical adsorption theories and thermodynamic constraints into the learning process. It uses over 2,200 sorption measurements and derives 62 thermodynamically meaningful features, with a structured training approach to enforce physical accuracy and stability.
AI.result: The optimized PINN achieves high accuracy (R²=0.9544, RMSE=0.0484 mmol/g, MAE=0.0231 mmol/g), with strong monotonicity and zero non-physical predictions. It also shows a 10-15% improvement in generalization across lithologies compared to random forest models.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that embedding physical principles into machine learning models enhances predictive accuracy and cross-lithology generalization, making the framework valuable for hydrogen storage evaluation and geological applications.

title: Fully Spiking Neural Networks with Target Awareness for Energy-Efficient UAV Tracking
summary: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), characterized by their event-driven computation and low power consumption, have shown great potential for energy-efficient visual tracking on unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). However, existing efficient SNN-based trackers heavily rely on costly event cameras, limiting their deployment on UAVs. To address this limitation, we propose STATrack, an efficient fully spiking neural network framework for UAV visual tracking using RGB inputs only. To the best of our knowledge, this work is the first to investigate spiking neural networks for UAV visual tracking tasks. To mitigate the weakening of target features by background tokens, we propose adaptively maximizing the mutual information between templates and features. Extensive experiments on four widely used UAV tracking benchmarks demonstrate that STATrack achieves competitive tracking performance while maintaining low energy consumption.

AI.tldr: STATrack is an efficient Spiking Neural Network-based tracker for UAV visual tracking using RGB inputs instead of costly event cameras, achieving competitive tracking performance and low energy use.
AI.motivation: Efficient UAV visual tracking requires methods that are both high-performing and energy-efficient, but existing techniques rely heavily on expensive event cameras, limiting their practicality.
AI.method: STATrack employs a fully Spiking Neural Network approach that uses RGB input for UAV visual tracking. It incorporates mutual information optimization between templates and features to enhance target feature retention.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations on four UAV tracking benchmarks show that STATrack performs competitively compared to alternative trackers while consuming less energy.
AI.conclusion: STATrack demonstrates the feasibility and efficiency of using RGB-only Spiking Neural Networks in UAV visual tracking, paving the way for more practical and energy-efficient applications.

title: Learning to Focus and Precise Cropping: A Reinforcement Learning Framework with Information Gaps and Grounding Loss for MLLMs
summary: To enhance the perception and reasoning capabilities of multimodal large language models in complex visual scenes, recent research has introduced agent-based workflows. In these works, MLLMs autonomously utilize image cropping tool to analyze regions of interest for question answering. While existing training strategies, such as those employing supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning, have made significant progress, our empirical analysis reveals a key limitation. We demonstrate the model's strong reliance on global input and its weak dependence on the details within the cropped region. To address this issue, we propose a novel two-stage reinforcement learning framework that does not require trajectory supervision. In the first stage, we introduce the ``Information Gap" mechanism by adjusting the granularity of the global image. This mechanism trains the model to answer questions by focusing on cropped key regions, driven by the information gain these regions provide. The second stage further enhances cropping precision by incorporating a grounding loss, using a small number of bounding box annotations. Experiments show that our method significantly enhances the model's attention to cropped regions, enabling it to achieve state-of-the-art performance on high-resolution visual question-answering benchmarks. Our method provides a more efficient approach for perceiving and reasoning fine-grained details in MLLMs. Code is available at: https://github.com/XuanPu-Z/LFPC.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a two-stage reinforcement learning framework to improve multimodal large language models' (MLLMs) perception and reasoning in complex visual scenes, focusing on enhancing attention to cropped regions in high-resolution visual question-answering tasks.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitation of existing MLLMs that heavily depend on global input and insufficiently utilize details within cropped regions for answering questions.
